Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Other obligations authorized by the lease

jurisdictions.title: Alabama Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Ala. Code § 35-9A-201

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline:
  * If landlord makes no deductions: 35 days after tenancy ends
  * If landlord makes deductions: 60 days after tenancy ends and surrender of premises
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Not specifically required by state statute.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to comply with return requirements, tenant may recover actual damages and attorney's fees.
- Abandonment: If tenant abandons premises, landlord may retain deposit to cover unpaid rent.
- No statutory cap means landlords can charge market-rate deposits of any amount.
Ala. Code § 35-9A-201: 'The landlord may not withhold any portion of a security deposit unless the landlord provides the tenant with an itemized list of deductions within sixty days after termination of the tenancy.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Ala. Code §§ 35-9A-421 to 35-9A-423

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: 7 days written notice to quit before filing. Tenant may pay overdue rent to reinstate tenancy within 7 days.
- Lease violation: 14 days written notice specifying violation and allowing tenant to cure.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT use self-help methods; must obtain court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks; contested cases may take months.
- Court process: Landlord files in district or circuit court; summons served; hearing scheduled.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, material noncompliance, end of lease term, nuisance.
- Failure to comply: If landlord proceeds without proper notice, court may dismiss the case.
Ala. Code § 35-9A-421: Governs landlord's right to recover possession for nonpayment of rent after proper notice.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Ala. Code §§ 35-9A-201 to 35-9A-304

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Alabama law recognizes landlord obligations to maintain habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with applicable building codes; keep premises in good repair; maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; provide garbage collection.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: NOT ALLOWED under Alabama law.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant cannot unilaterally withhold rent.
  3. Termination: If landlord fails to maintain habitable premises, tenant may terminate the rental agreement after 14 days written notice and landlord's failure to repair.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action to enforce landlord's obligations.
- Retaliatory eviction: NOT specifically prohibited by state statute.
- Rent control: None. Ala. Code § 35-9A-101 et seq. does not provide for rent control. State law preempts local rent control ordinances.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local and state building codes.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Ala. Code § 35-9A-201

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Ala. Code § 35-9A-302

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Alabama law requires 'reasonable notice' but does not specify minimum hours.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver packages, or provide services.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
Ala. Code § 35-9A-302: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only: (1) at reasonable times; (2) in a reasonable manner; (3) after proper notice.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Ala. Code §§ 35-9A-101 to 35-9A-603

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Alabama. State law preempts local governments from enacting rent control.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Landlord may raise rent by any amount with proper notice.

allowable_deductions: Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Unpaid rent

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; no mandatory duty to mitigate

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Arizona Revised Statutes (A.R.S.) Title 33, Chapter 10 – Arizona Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (§§ 33-1301 through 33-1381)
jurisdictions.notes: Arizona is a landlord-friendly state with no statewide rent control and strong preemption of local ordinances. A.R.S. §33-1329 explicitly prohibits local governments from enacting rent control. Deposits capped at 1.5 months rent. 5-day notice for nonpayment.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
A.R.S. § 33-1321. SECURITY DEPOSITS.

A. A landlord shall not require a security deposit, however denominated, in excess of one and one-half (1.5) month's rent. This subsection does not prohibit a tenant from voluntarily paying more than one and one-half month's rent as a deposit.

B. All nonrefundable fees or deposits shall be stated as such and the purpose shall be stated in writing by the landlord. Any fees or deposits not designated as nonrefundable shall be refundable.

C. At move-in, the landlord shall provide the tenant with: (1) a signed copy of the lease; (2) a move-in inspection checklist documenting existing damages; and (3) written notice of the tenant's right to be present at the move-out inspection. Upon request, the landlord shall notify the tenant of the time of the move-out inspection.

D. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord may apply the security deposit and prepaid rent against: (1) all rent due; and (2) all costs, damages and fees as specified in the signed lease or this chapter, including damages for tenant's noncompliance per §33-1341. Within 14 business days (excluding Saturday, Sunday, and legal holidays) after the tenant has vacated, the landlord shall provide an itemized list of all deductions from the deposit together with any remaining balance due to the tenant. [Mailed to tenant's last known address.] If the tenant does not dispute the itemization within 60 days, the amounts are final.

E. If the landlord fails to comply with subsection D, the tenant may recover the property and money owed plus damages equal to TWICE the amount wrongfully withheld.

F. This section does not preclude any party from recovering other damages under this chapter.

G. During the tenancy, the landlord may use the refundable deposit or other refundable funds pursuant to the property management agreement. At end of tenancy, all refundable deposits must be returned per this section.

H. Upon termination of the landlord's interest, the new holder of the landlord's interest is bound by this section.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
A.R.S. § 33-1368. NONCOMPLIANCE WITH RENTAL AGREEMENT BY TENANT; FAILURE TO PAY RENT.

(A) Except as provided in subsection (C) of this section, if there is a material noncompliance by the tenant with the rental agreement, the landlord may deliver a written notice to the tenant specifying the acts and omissions constituting the breach and that the rental agreement will terminate upon a date not less than ten (10) days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied.

(B) If there is a material and irreparable breach of the rental agreement by the tenant, the landlord may deliver a written notice to the tenant specifying the acts constituting the breach and that the rental agreement is terminated on a date not less than three (3) immediately consecutive calendar days after receipt of the notice.

Irreparable breaches include: inflicting serious bodily harm; possessing a weapon prohibited by statute; engaging in or threatening drug-related activity; making misrepresentation to obtain the tenancy; prostitution or other unlawful behavior.

(C) If rent is unpaid when due and the tenant fails to pay within five (5) days after written notice by the landlord of nonpayment and intent to terminate, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement.

A.R.S. § 33-1375. PERIODIC TENANCY; HOLDOVER REMEDIES.
Month-to-month: either party may terminate with at least 30 days written notice.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
A.R.S. § 33-1343. ACCESS.

A. The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter into the dwelling unit in order to inspect the premises; make necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations or improvements; supply necessary or agreed services; or exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers or contractors.

B. If the tenant has given the landlord a service or repair request under §33-1341(8), this constitutes the tenant's permission for the landlord to enter the dwelling unit pursuant to subsection D of this section SOLELY for the purpose of addressing the service or repair request, and the tenant waives the right to receive separate notice required by subsection D for that specific entry.

C. The landlord may enter the dwelling unit WITHOUT the tenant's consent in case of emergency.

D. The landlord shall not abuse the right of access or use it to harass the tenant. Except in case of emergency, or as impracticable, the landlord shall give the tenant at least TWO (2) days' notice of the landlord's intent to enter and may enter only at reasonable times.

E. The landlord has no other right of access except by court order, as permitted by §§33-1369 and 33-1370, or if the tenant has surrendered the premises.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section:
A.R.S. § 33-1329. RENT REGULATION; AUTHORIZATION.
A city, town or other governmental entity shall not enact, maintain or enforce any ordinance or regulation that would control the amount of rent charged for private residential or commercial property. Arizona has preempted all local rent control since 1981.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Arizona has no statewide cap on late fees. Late fees must be stated in the lease. Common practice: 5% of monthly rent after a 5-day grace period. The statute does not specify a mandatory grace period.

A.R.S. § 33-1314(C): The rental agreement shall not provide for late payment charges based upon a percentage of the outstanding balance when such charges are not reasonable and are not an estimate of actual damages incurred.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
A.R.S. § 33-1322. DISCLOSURE AND FURNISHING OF RENTAL AGREEMENT.
(A) Landlord shall provide tenant with: (1) a copy of the rental agreement; (2) names, addresses and telephone numbers of all persons authorized to manage the premises and receive notices; (3) written disclosure of the move-in inspection process.

A.R.S. § 33-1314.01: UTILITY BILLING DISCLOSURE. If utilities are billed based on ratio utility billing (RUBS), landlord must disclose the calculation method in writing.

Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.

A.R.S. § 33-1319. BED BUGS. Landlord must provide information about bed bug infestation history and prevention.

Flood zone: No specific landlord-tenant statute; however recommended practice.

other_provisions.domestic_violence_termination: A.R.S. § 33-1318: Victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, or unauthorized disclosure of sexual images may terminate lease with 30 days written notice and documentation (e.g., protective order, police report, medical report, affidavit).
other_provisions.law_enforcement_termination: A.R.S. § 33-1318.01: Law enforcement officers may terminate lease under certain duty-related circumstances.
other_provisions.retaliation: A.R.S. § 33-1381: Landlord shall not retaliate against tenant for good faith complaints to government agencies, habitability requests, or tenant organization membership. Presumption of retaliation for adverse action within 12 months of protected activity.
other_provisions.abandonment: A.R.S. § 33-1370: If tenant abandons unit, landlord must make reasonable efforts to re-rent; personal property remaining must be handled per abandonment procedures.
other_provisions.pet_policy: No statewide restriction on pet deposits or pet fees, but combined deposits cannot exceed 1.5 months rent per §33-1321. Lease must specify pet policy.
other_provisions.smoke_alarms: Governed by building codes; landlord must maintain in working order.
other_provisions.mold: Covered under habitability warranty (§33-1324); landlord must remediate.

security_deposit.notes: Nonrefundable fees must be designated in writing. AZ also prohibits security deposits for satellite dishes/antennae.

eviction.court_process_summary: Forcible detainer action in justice court

rent_control.notes: Arizona prohibits local rent control (state preemption). No statewide rent control.

landlord_entry.notes: Landlord may enter for repairs, inspections, or to show unit with reasonable notice

habitability.notes: Landlord must maintain fit and habitable premises. Tenant may terminate lease if premises become uninhabitable.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: California Civil Code §§ 1940–1954.1 (Hiring of Real Property); Civil Code §§ 1946–1946.1 (Termination); Civil Code § 1950.5 (Security Deposits); AB 1482 (Tenant Protection Act of 2019 – Rent Cap & Just Cause) – Civil Code §§ 1946.2, 1947.12
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2026 (multiple 2025 and 2026 amendments including AB 628 re habitability)
jurisdictions.notes: California has one of the most tenant-protective laws in the US. AB 1482 (2020) imposes statewide rent cap (5% + CPI, max 10%) and Just Cause eviction on most rentals. SB 567 (2024) strengthened owner move-in eviction rules. AB 12 (2024) reduced security deposit cap from 2 months to 1 month rent (effective April 1, 2024). AB 2801 (2024) added security deposit photo documentation requirements. AB 628 (2025) expanded habitability requirements.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Cal. Civil Code § 1950.5. SECURITY DEPOSITS.

(b) The landlord may claim of the security deposit only those amounts as are reasonably necessary for the following purposes:
(1) The compensation of the landlord for the tenant's default in the payment of rent.
(2) The repair of damages to the premises, exclusive of ordinary wear and tear, caused by the tenant or by a guest or licensee of the tenant.
(3) The cleaning of the premises upon termination of the tenancy necessary to return the unit to the same level of cleanliness it was in at the inception of the tenancy.
(4) To remedy future defaults by the tenant in any obligation under the rental agreement to restore, replace, or return personal property or appurtenances.

(c) AB 12 (2024) effective April 1, 2024: Maximum security deposit reduced to ONE MONTH'S RENT for all tenants (previously 2 months unfurnished, 3 months furnished). Exception: Small landlords owning no more than 2 residential rental properties with no more than 4 total units may collect up to 2 months' rent.

(l) No later than 21 calendar days after the tenant has vacated the premises, the landlord shall furnish the tenant, by personal delivery or by first-class mail, postage prepaid, a copy of an itemized statement indicating the basis for, and the amount of, any security received and the disposition of the security, and shall return any remaining portion of the security to the tenant.

(m) AB 2801 (2024): Upon commencement of tenancy, landlord must take photographs of the unit. Before deducting for repairs, landlord must take photographs of the claimed damage. At completion of repairs, photographs must be taken. Photos must be provided with itemized statement.

(n) Upon a claim by the tenant that the landlord acted in bad faith in claiming all or any portion of a security deposit, the landlord shall have the burden of proving good faith. The court shall award damages not to exceed twice the amount of the security deposit in addition to actual damages.

(p) The landlord, or the landlord's agent, shall provide the tenant with an itemized statement of charges if the landlord deducts any amounts from the deposit.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Cal. Civil Code § 1946. MONTH-TO-MONTH TERMINATION. A hiring of real property, for a term not specified by the parties, is deemed to be renewed as stated in Section 1945, at the end of the term implied by law unless one of the parties gives written notice to the other of that party's intention to terminate the same, at least as long before the expiration thereof as the term of the hiring itself, not exceeding 30 days; provided, however, that as to tenancies from month-to-month either of the parties may terminate the same by giving at least 30 days' written notice thereof at any time and the rent shall be due and payable to and including the date of termination. [30-day notice for tenancies <1 year; 60-day notice for tenancies ≥1 year per §1946.1]

Cal. Civil Code § 1946.2. JUST CAUSE EVICTION (AB 1482, effective Jan 1 2020).
(a) After a tenant has continuously and lawfully occupied a residential real property for 12 months, the owner of the residential real property shall not terminate the tenancy without just cause.
(b) For purposes of this section, 'just cause' includes either of the following:
(1) At-fault just cause, which is any of the following:
(A) Default in the payment of rent.
(B) A breach of a material term of the lease as described in paragraph (3) of Section 1161 of the Code of Civil Procedure.
(C) Maintaining, committing, or permitting the maintenance or commission of a nuisance.
(D) Committing waste.
(E) The tenant had a written lease that terminated on or after January 1, 2020, and after a written request or demand from the owner, the tenant has refused to execute a written extension or renewal of the lease for an additional term of similar duration with similar provisions.
(F) Criminal activity by the tenant on the residential real property, including any common areas, or any criminal activity or criminal threat directed at any owner or agent of the owner of the residential real property.
(G) Assigning or subletting the premises in violation of the tenant's lease.
(H) The tenant's refusal to allow the owner to enter the residential real property as authorized by Sections 1101.5 and 1954.
(I) Using the premises for an unlawful purpose.
(J) The employee, agent, or licensee's failure to vacate after their termination.
(K) When the tenant fails to deliver possession of the residential real property after providing the owner written notice as provided in Section 1946 or 1946.1.
(2) No-fault just cause, which is any of the following:
(A) Intent to occupy the residential real property by the owner or their spouse, domestic partner, children, grandchildren, parents, or grandparents.
(B) Withdrawal of the residential real property from the rental market.
(C) The owner complying with any of the following: (i) An order issued by a government agency or court relating to habitability that necessitates vacating the residential real property; or (ii) An order issued by a government agency or court to vacate the residential real property.
(D) Intent to demolish or to substantially remodel the residential real property.
(c) For a no-fault just cause: owner must provide relocation assistance equal to ONE MONTH'S RENT or waive final month's rent.
(d) Exempt properties: Single-family homes where owner provided required written notice; condos sold separately; properties built within last 15 years; owner-occupied duplex; certain licensed/regulated housing.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Cal. Civil Code § 1954. WHEN LANDLORD MAY ENTER DWELLING UNIT.

(a) A landlord may enter the dwelling unit only in the following cases:
(1) In case of emergency.
(2) To make necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations or improvements, supply necessary or agreed services, or exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors or to make an inspection pursuant to subdivision (f) of Section 1950.5.
(3) When the tenant has abandoned or surrendered the premises.
(4) Pursuant to court order.
(b) Except in cases of emergency or when the tenant has abandoned or surrendered the premises, entry may not be made during other than normal business hours unless the tenant consents to entry during other than normal business hours at the time of entry.
(c) The landlord may not abuse the right of access or use it to harass the tenant.
(d)(1) Except as provided in subdivision (e), or as provided in paragraph (2) or (3), the landlord shall give the tenant reasonable notice in writing before entering and shall enter only during normal business hours. Twenty-four hours shall be presumed to be reasonable notice in absence of evidence to the contrary. The notice shall include the date, approximate time, and purpose of the entry.
(2) If the purpose of the entry is to exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, the notice may be given orally, in person or by telephone, if the landlord or his or her agent has notified the tenant in writing within 120 days of the oral notice that the property is for sale and that the landlord or agent may contact the tenant orally for the purpose described above. Twenty-four hours is presumed reasonable notice.
(3) The tenant and the landlord may agree orally to an entry for agreed services or repairs if the date and time of entry are within one week of the agreement.
(e) No notice of entry is required: (1) to respond to an emergency; (2) the tenant is present and consents to the entry; or (3) the tenant has abandoned or surrendered the unit.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Cal. Civil Code § 1941. LESSOR TO MAINTAIN PREMISES. The lessor of a building intended for the occupation of human beings must, in the absence of an agreement to the contrary, put it into a condition fit for such occupation, and repair all subsequent dilapidations thereof, which render it untenantable, except such as are mentioned in Section 1929.

Cal. Civil Code § 1941.1. UNTENANTABLE DWELLING. A dwelling shall be deemed untenantable for the purposes of Section 1941 if it substantially lacks any of the following affirmative standard characteristics or is a residential unit described in Section 17920.3 of the Health and Safety Code:
(a) Effective waterproofing and weather protection of roof and exterior walls, including unbroken windows and doors.
(b) Plumbing or gas facilities that conformed to applicable law in effect at the time of installation, maintained in good working order.
(c) A water supply approved under applicable law that is under the control of the tenant, capable of producing hot and cold running water, or a system that is under the control of the landlord, that produces hot and cold running water, furnished to appropriate fixtures, and connected to a sewage disposal system approved under applicable law.
(d) Heating facilities that conformed with applicable law at the time of installation, maintained in good working order.
(e) Electrical lighting, with wiring and electrical equipment that conformed with applicable law at the time of installation, maintained in good working order.
(f) Building, grounds, and appurtenances at the time of the commencement of the lease or rental agreement, and all areas under control of the landlord, kept in every part clean, sanitary, and free from all accumulations of debris, filth, rubbish, garbage, rodents, and vermin.
(g) An adequate number of appropriate receptacles for garbage and rubbish, in clean condition and good repair at the time of the commencement of the lease or rental agreement, with the landlord providing appropriate serviceable receptacles thereafter and being responsible for the clean condition and good repair of such receptacles under his or her control.
(h) Floors, stairways, and railings maintained in good repair.
(i) A locking mail receptacle for each residential unit in a residential hotel as required by Section 17958.3 of the Health and Safety Code.
(j) [Effective January 1, 2026 per AB 628] A working stove in good condition and repair. A landlord is not required to provide a stove that has been recalled.
(k) [Effective January 1, 2026 per AB 628] A working refrigerator in good condition and repair. A landlord is not required to provide a refrigerator that has been recalled. The landlord and tenant may enter into a written agreement in which the tenant agrees to provide their own refrigerator.

Cal. Civil Code § 1942. REPAIR BY LESSEE; DEDUCTION FROM RENT. (a) If within a reasonable time after written or oral notice to the landlord or his agent, as defined in subdivision (a) of Section 1962, of dilapidations which the landlord ought to repair, the landlord neglects to do so, the tenant may repair the same himself, where the cost of such repairs does not exceed one month's rent of the premises, and deduct the expenses of such repairs from the rent when due, or the tenant may vacate the premises, in which case the tenant shall be discharged from further payment of rent, or performance of other conditions as of the date of vacating the premises. The tenant's remedy under this subdivision is not available more than twice in any 12-month period.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section:
Cal. Civil Code § 1947.12. RENT CAP (AB 1482 Tenant Protection Act of 2019).
(a)(1) An owner of residential real property shall not, over the course of any 12-month period, increase the gross rental rate for a dwelling or a unit more than 5 percent plus the percentage change in the cost of living, or 10 percent, whichever is lower, of the lowest gross rental rate charged for that dwelling or unit at any time during the 12 months prior to the effective date of the increase.
(a)(2) If the same tenant has been in occupancy of a dwelling or unit for 24 months or more, the maximum increase applies to the lowest rental rate in effect for the unit in the 12 months before the increase.
(b) Exemptions include: (1) housing built within 15 years of the increase date; (2) single-family homes and condos where owner has provided required written notice; (3) owner-occupied properties with no more than 2 units; (4) dormitories, certain licensed/regulated housing.
(c) Rent increases subject to local stricter ordinances (e.g., Los Angeles, San Francisco, Oakland have more protective rent control).
jurisdictions.late_fees_section: California has no specific statewide late fee cap. Late fees must be reasonable and represent a 'liquidated damages' estimate. Courts typically look skeptically at late fees exceeding 5-10% of monthly rent. Three-day notice to pay or quit may be served for nonpayment (CCP §1161(2)).
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
Required disclosures under California law:
1. §1940.7: Military ordnance location within 1 mile – written disclosure required if landlord has actual knowledge.
2. §1940.6: Demolition permit application – must disclose to prospective and current tenants.
3. §1940.9: Shared utilities – must disclose and establish written agreement for payment allocation.
4. Federal lead paint disclosure: 42 U.S.C. §4852d – required for pre-1978 housing.
5. §1962: Landlord must provide written notice of owner/agent name and address.
6. §1947.3: Right to pay rent by personal check.
7. Bedbug disclosure: §1954.603–§1954.605: landlord must provide bedbug info to tenants; inspection/remediation procedures.
8. §1940.8.5: Pesticide disclosure – 24-hour advance written notice if landlord applies pesticide.
9. Mold: SB 655 – landlord must remediate mold affecting habitability; must disclose known mold.
10. Flood zone: AB 1166 (2024) – landlord must disclose if property is in high or very high fire hazard severity zone or special flood hazard area.

other_provisions.domestic_violence: §1946.7: Victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, human trafficking, or elder/dependent adult abuse may terminate lease with 14 days written notice and supporting documentation.
other_provisions.retaliation: §1942.5: Retaliatory eviction prohibited. Retaliation presumed if adverse action within 180 days of tenant's protected activity (complaint to government agency, habitability complaint, repair request, tenant organizing). Landlord liable for actual damages + punitive damages of 1-3 months rent.
other_provisions.subletting: §1995.010–§1995.340: General rules for assignment/subletting; residential tenant may not sublet without landlord consent unless lease is silent.
other_provisions.habitability_defense: §1942: Tenant may raise habitability as defense to unlawful detainer; rent withholding allowed if landlord fails to repair.
other_provisions.early_termination_just_cause: Under AB 1482, no-fault eviction requires relocation assistance equal to 1 month's rent.
other_provisions.AB_1482_effective_date: AB 1482 took effect January 1, 2020. Sunset extended indefinitely as of AB 1188 (2021).
other_provisions.security_deposit_AB_12: AB 12 effective April 1, 2024: Security deposit cap reduced to 1 month's rent for most landlords.
other_provisions.pet_deposits: Pet deposits count toward the total security deposit cap under §1950.5.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Colorado Revised Statutes (C.R.S.) Title 38, Article 12 – Tenants and Landlords (§§ 38-12-101 through 38-12-1400+)
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (SB 24-094 effective August 2024; HB 24-1098 effective July 2024; multiple 2023 amendments still in effect)
jurisdictions.notes: Colorado has seen major tenant protections added since 2021. SB 173 (2021) added warranty of habitability. HB 1121 (2022) added just cause eviction statewide for tenants with >12 months occupancy. SB 094 (2024) expanded protections. C.R.S. 38-12-301 prohibits local rent control statewide (since 1981). No statewide rent control.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
C.R.S. § 38-12-102.5. SECURITY DEPOSIT LIMITS.
Effective January 1, 2024: Security deposit may not exceed TWO MONTHS' RENT for standard residential tenancies. [Previously no cap.]

C.R.S. § 38-12-103. RETURN OF SECURITY DEPOSIT.
(1) A landlord shall, within one month after the termination of the lease or surrender and acceptance of the premises, whichever occurs last, return to the tenant the full security deposit deposited with the landlord by the tenant, unless the landlord elects to withhold all or any portion of the security deposit for the reasons set forth in this section. If the lease agreement specifies a longer period, the landlord may withhold for up to 60 days.
(2) The landlord shall not retain a security deposit or any portion thereof for conditions that are the result of normal wear and tear.
(3) In the event that actual cause exists for retaining any portion of the security deposit, the landlord shall provide the tenant with a written statement listing the exact reasons for the retention of any portion of the security deposit. If the landlord fails to provide a written statement and to return the portion of the deposit to which the tenant is entitled within the time period specified in subsection (1) of this section, the landlord forfeits the right to withhold any portion of the security deposit.
(4) The landlord may withhold from the security deposit only such amounts as are reasonably necessary for the following purposes: (a) to remedy tenant's default in the payment of rent; (b) to repair damage to the premises caused by the tenant, reasonable wear and tear excluded; (c) to pay the cost of storing or otherwise disposing of the tenant's unclaimed property; (d) to pay the amount of unpaid utility fees; and (e) for cleaning of the unit that is agreed upon in the lease.
(5) In any court action brought by a tenant under this section, the landlord shall bear the burden of proving that his withholding of the security deposit or any portion of it was not wrongful.
(6) Wrongful withholding: If the landlord wrongfully withholds all or any portion of the security deposit, the landlord shall be liable for treble (3x) the amount wrongfully withheld plus court costs and reasonable attorney fees.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
C.R.S. § 38-12-503. WARRANTY OF HABITABILITY.
(1) In every rental agreement, the landlord is deemed to warrant that the residential premises are fit for human habitation.
(2) A landlord breaches the warranty of habitability if:
(a) The residential premises substantially lack any of the following characteristics or conditions: (I) adequate weatherproofing; (II) adequate heating facilities; (III) adequate plumbing, including hot and cold running water; (IV) adequate electrical service; (V) adequate ventilation; (VI) appropriate extermination of pests and vermin; (VII) clean condition of building and grounds; (VIII) adequate garbage and rubbish storage; (IX) adequate exit facilities; (X) adequate natural lighting, ventilation, or electricity in every room; (XI) required fire safety systems; (XII) absence of toxic hazards including lead paint, carbon monoxide, etc.; (XIII) restoration of unit after disaster per public health standards; AND
(b) The landlord receives notice and does not begin remediation within: (I) 24 hours – if condition 'materially and substantially' affects health or safety; (II) 96 hours – if unit is generally untenantable.

(2.5) Within 24 hours of receiving tenant notice, landlord must respond acknowledging the notice and provide estimated timeline for repair.

(2.7) Landlord must bear all costs of repair to meet habitability standards.

(3) Exception: Tenant caused the condition through misconduct. Exception does not apply if condition results from domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking.

(4) If tenant must temporarily vacate for repairs, landlord must provide comparable alternative housing or hotel at no cost to tenant.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section:
C.R.S. § 38-12-301. PROHIBITION ON RENT CONTROL.
A county or municipality shall not enact any ordinance or resolution that would control the rent on private residential property. Colorado has had a statewide ban on local rent control since 1981.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
C.R.S. § 38-12-105. LATE FEES.
(1) Late fee: May not exceed:
(a) $50 or 5% of the past-due rent, whichever is greater, for the first late fee;
(b) $25 or 2% of the remaining past-due rent per month, for subsequent late fee on same past-due amount.
(2) 7-day grace period: Landlord may not charge a late fee until rent is past due for more than 7 days.
(3) Late fees must be stated in the lease agreement.
(4) Landlord may not charge multiple late fees for the same delinquency.
(5) Prohibition on administrative fees or other fees not permitted by statute.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
C.R.S. § 38-12-803. RADON DISCLOSURE.
Landlord must disclose whether premises has been tested for radon and results of any testing.

C.R.S. § 38-12-1001 et seq. BED BUG DISCLOSURE. Landlord must disclose any known bed bug infestation within 30 days of receiving notice.

C.R.S. § 38-12-904. APPLICATION FEE. Landlord may only charge actual cost of screening, not to exceed $75. If unit is not available, fee must be refunded.

Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.

C.R.S. § 38-12-802: Written lease must include tenant's rights and responsibilities under Colorado law (summary required if lease ≥ 5 pages).

other_provisions.just_cause_2022: HB 1121 (2022) established first-in-state just cause eviction protections for tenants with >12 months continuous occupancy. Represents major shift from traditional Colorado landlord-friendly law.
other_provisions.security_deposit_cap_2024: SB 094 (2024) added 2-month security deposit cap, effective January 1, 2024.
other_provisions.91_day_notice_2024: HB 1098 (2024): Month-to-month tenancy termination requires 91 days advance notice (up from 21 days).
other_provisions.domestic_violence: C.R.S. §38-12-402: Domestic violence victim may terminate lease early with 7-day notice and documentation.
other_provisions.retaliation: C.R.S. §38-12-509: Anti-retaliation. 30-day presumption – adverse action within 30 days of protected tenant activity presumed retaliatory.
other_provisions.pet_restrictions: C.R.S. §38-12-106: Pet deposits limited to 2 months rent (combined with security deposit cap).
other_provisions.source_of_income: C.R.S. §38-12-1201: Landlord may not discriminate against tenant based on source of income (including Section 8/Housing Choice Vouchers), effective January 1, 2023.
other_provisions.electric_vehicles: C.R.S. §38-12-601: Tenant has right to install EV charging in parking space at own expense.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Florida Statutes Title VI, Chapter 83 Part II – Residential Tenancies (§§ 83.40–83.683)
jurisdictions.notes: Florida has NO statewide rent control. §83.425 preempts all local rent control ordinances. SB 280 (2023) reinforced preemption and invalidated all local rent control measures. Florida is a landlord-friendly state with specific 3-day/7-day notice requirements.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Fla. Stat. § 83.49. DEPOSIT MONEY OR ADVANCE RENT; DUTY OF LANDLORD AND TENANT.

(1) Whenever money is deposited or advanced by a tenant on a rental agreement as security for performance of the rental agreement or as advance rent for other than the next immediate rental period, the landlord or the landlord's agent shall either:
(a) Hold the total amount of such money in a separate non-interest-bearing account in a Florida banking institution for the benefit of the tenant or tenants. The landlord shall not commingle such moneys with any other funds of the landlord or hypothecate, pledge, or in any way make use of such moneys until such moneys are actually due the landlord;
(b) Hold the total amount of such money in a separate interest-bearing account in a Florida banking institution for the benefit of the tenant or tenants, in which case the tenant shall receive and collect interest in an amount of at least 75 percent of the annualized average interest rate payable on such account or interest at the rate of 5 percent per year, simple interest, whichever the landlord elects; or
(c) Post a surety bond, executed by the landlord as principal and a surety company authorized and licensed in the state as surety, with the clerk of the circuit court in the county in which the dwelling unit is located. The bond shall be in the total amount of the security deposits and advance rent held on behalf of tenants or $50,000, whichever is less. Landlords with offices in five or more counties may post the bond with the Secretary of State, limited to $250,000.

(2) The landlord shall, within 30 days of receipt of advance rent or a security deposit, give written notice to the tenant which shall include disclosure of the advance rent or deposit, the name and address of the depository or surety company, whether the tenant is entitled to interest, and a statement that reads substantially as follows: 'Your lease requires payment of certain deposits. The landlord may transfer the deposit to any new owner or may return the deposit to you. If the landlord transfers the deposit, the new owner must notify you within 30 days of the transfer.'

(3)(a) Upon termination of the tenancy, money held as security may be applied to the payment of accrued rent and the amount of damages that the landlord has suffered by reason of the tenant's noncompliance with Section 83.52, all as itemized by the landlord in a written notice mailed to the tenant, together with the amount due, within 15 days after termination of the tenancy and delivery of possession and demand by the tenant. However, if the landlord intends to impose a claim on the deposit, the landlord shall have 30 days to give the tenant written notice by certified mail to the tenant's last known mailing address of the landlord's intention to impose a claim on the deposit and the reason for imposing the claim.
(b) If the landlord fails to give the required notice within the 30-day period, he or she forfeits the right to impose a claim upon the security deposit and shall return the security deposit to the tenant, together with interest if otherwise required.
(c) Unless the tenant objects to the imposition of the landlord's claim or the amount thereof within 15 days after receipt of the landlord's notice of intention to impose a claim, the landlord may then deduct the amount of his or her claim and shall remit the balance of the deposit to the tenant within 30 days after the date of such notice of intention to impose a claim for damages.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Fla. Stat. § 83.56. TERMINATION OF RENTAL AGREEMENT.

(1) If the landlord materially fails to comply with s. 83.51(1) or material provisions of the rental agreement within 7 days after delivery of written notice by the tenant specifying the noncompliance and indicating the intention of the tenant to terminate the rental agreement by reason thereof, the tenant may terminate the rental agreement.

(2) If the tenant materially fails to comply with s. 83.52 or material provisions of the rental agreement, other than a failure to pay rent, or reasonably conducts himself or herself in a manner that is found to be a nuisance, including, but not limited to, destruction, damage, injury, or misuse of the landlord's or other tenants' property, unreasonable disturbances, or drug-related activity, within 7 days after delivery of written notice by the landlord specifying the noncompliance, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement.

(3) If the tenant fails to pay rent when due and the default continues for 3 days (excluding Saturday, Sunday, and legal holidays) after delivery of written demand by the landlord for payment of the rent or possession of the premises, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement. The notice shall:
'You are hereby notified that you are indebted to me in the sum of ___ dollars for the rent and use of the premises [address of leased premises, including county], Florida, now occupied by you and that I demand payment of the rent or possession of the premises within 3 days (excluding Saturday, Sunday, and legal holidays) from the date of delivery of this notice, to wit: on or before the ___ day of ___, (year).
(Landlord's name, address and telephone number)'

Fla. Stat. § 83.57. TERMINATION OF TENANCY WITHOUT SPECIFIC TERM.
(1) Month-to-month: 15 days written notice;
(2) Week-to-week: 7 days written notice;
(3) Quarter-to-quarter: 30 days written notice;
(4) Year-to-year: 60 days written notice.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Fla. Stat. § 83.53. LANDLORD'S ACCESS TO DWELLING UNIT.

(1) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter the dwelling unit from time to time in order to inspect the premises; make necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; supply agreed services; or exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors.

(2) The landlord may enter the dwelling unit at any time for the protection or preservation of the premises. The landlord may enter the dwelling unit upon reasonable notice to the tenant and at a reasonable time for the purpose of repair of the premises. 'Reasonable notice' for the purpose of repair is notice given at least 12 hours prior to the entry, and 'reasonable time' for the purpose of repair shall be between the hours of 7:30 a.m. and 8:00 p.m. The landlord may enter the dwelling unit when necessary for the protection or preservation of the premises, or upon:
(a) Consent of the tenant;
(b) In case of emergency;
(c) When the tenant unreasonably withholds consent; or
(d) If the tenant is absent from the premises for a period of time equal to one-half the time for periodic rental payments. However, if the tenant has given the landlord notice of an anticipated extended absence from the premises, the landlord may enter only with the consent of the tenant or for the protection or preservation of the premises.

(3) The landlord shall not abuse the right of access or use it to harass the tenant.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Fla. Stat. § 83.425. PREEMPTION. The regulation of residential tenancies, the landlord-tenant relationship, and all other matters covered under this part are preempted to the state. This section supersedes any local ordinance in conflict with this part, including any local government ordinance that regulates or limits the amount of rent charged for dwelling units. Florida has NO rent control. All local rent stabilization measures were invalidated.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section: Florida has no specific statute capping late fees. §83.808 (mobile homes) limits late fees. For standard residential, late fees are governed by lease terms. Industry practice: must be 'reasonable' and disclosed in lease. No mandatory grace period in statute (though 3-day nonpayment notice gives de facto grace).
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
§83.50: Landlord must disclose address where tenant can demand notices.
§83.49(2): Security deposit notice required within 30 days.
Lead paint disclosure: federal 42 U.S.C. §4852d (homes built before 1978).
Flood zone disclosure: Required for buyers but not tenants under state law (check local ordinances).
Bed bug history: No statewide statute.
Radon: Florida law requires radon disclosure in all contracts for sale and purchase of buildings.

other_provisions.retaliation: §83.64: Prohibited acts – Landlord may not retaliate against a tenant for good-faith complaints to a governmental agency, exercising rights under the rental agreement, or organizing a tenants' association. Retaliation presumed if adverse action taken within 1 year of tenant's protected activity.
other_provisions.prohibited_acts: §83.67: Landlord shall not: (1) prevent tenant from gaining access by any means other than court proceedings; (2) willfully terminate utilities; (3) remove outside doors, locks, roof, walls or windows; (4) remove tenant's personal property from dwelling; (5) influence tenant to vacate through intentional conduct. Violator liable for actual and consequential damages, or 3 months' rent, whichever is greater.
other_provisions.early_termination: §83.595: If tenant terminates prior to end of lease, landlord has obligation to make good faith effort to re-rent and apply newly received rents toward original tenant's balance.
other_provisions.military: §83.682: Active duty military may terminate lease with 30 days notice.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: §83.67 and Florida Safe Homes Act: Victim of domestic violence may terminate lease early with documentation.
other_provisions.nonrefundable_fees: §83.491: Landlord may collect a fee (in lieu of security deposit) if lease so provides; fee non-refundable.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Official Code of Georgia Annotated (O.C.G.A.) Title 44, Chapter 7 – Landlord and Tenant (§§ 44-7-1 through 44-7-99)
jurisdictions.notes: Georgia is one of the most landlord-friendly states. No rent control statewide. No security deposit cap (unless more than 10 rental units – escrow required). No just cause eviction requirement. Very limited tenant entry protections. Dispossessory (eviction) proceedings are streamlined and fast.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
O.C.G.A. § 44-7-30. DEFINITIONS. As used in this article:
(1) 'Security deposit' means all money, other than a rental payment, paid by a tenant to a landlord as a deposit for security of performance of the tenant's obligations under a rental agreement.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-30.1. MAXIMUM SECURITY DEPOSIT. Georgia has NO statutory cap on security deposits. A landlord may charge any amount.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-31. LANDLORD'S DUTIES REGARDING SECURITY DEPOSIT.
(a) The landlord shall deposit any security deposit received from a tenant in a trust or escrow account maintained only for the purpose of holding security deposits in a bank, savings institution, or credit union organized under the laws of this state or the United States. Deposits shall not be commingled with the personal funds of the landlord.
(b) Exception: If the landlord owns TEN OR FEWER rental units, the requirement to maintain an escrow account does NOT apply; however the landlord must still follow return procedures.
(c) The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written list of any existing damage to the premises prior to or at commencement of the tenancy. The tenant may acknowledge the list in writing, may note disagreements.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-33. MOVE-OUT INSPECTION; RETURN OF DEPOSIT.
(a) The tenant shall vacate the premises and give the landlord written notice of the forwarding address.
(b) Within THREE (3) business days after the tenant vacates, the landlord shall inspect the premises and compile a comprehensive list of damages.
(c) The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written itemized list of damages within 30 days after the tenant vacates or supplies forwarding address, whichever is later.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-34. RETURN OF SECURITY DEPOSIT.
(a) Within 30 days after obtaining possession of the premises, the landlord shall return to the tenant the full security deposit which was deposited by the tenant, less any sum properly withheld per §44-7-33.
(b) If the landlord withholds any portion, the landlord shall provide an itemized statement of deductions.
(c) Improper withholding: If the landlord wrongfully withholds any portion of the security deposit, the tenant may be awarded judgment for three times the amount wrongfully withheld plus reasonable attorney's fees.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-35. PENALTIES.
(a) If the landlord: (1) fails to return the deposit within the 30-day period; (2) fails to provide the itemized statement; or (3) improperly withholds a portion of the deposit, the tenant may bring an action for the deposit plus penalty damages equal to three times the amount wrongfully withheld plus reasonable attorney's fees.

Lawful deductions: (1) unpaid rent; (2) damage to premises beyond normal wear and tear; (3) costs to restore or replace personal property; (4) reasonable cleaning costs if unit left in unsatisfactory condition.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
O.C.G.A. § 44-7-50. DISPOSSESSORY PROCEEDINGS (EVICTION).
(a) Whenever a tenant holds possession of lands or tenements beyond the term for which they were rented to him or fails to pay the rent when it becomes due, or whenever a tenant at will or a tenant by sufferance fails to pay rent when it becomes due, the landlord may bring an action to recover possession.
(b) The landlord shall demand possession of the property before filing a dispossessory action. Typically, a written demand for possession (or pay rent or quit) serves as the notice.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-7. AT-WILL TENANCIES – NOTICE.
The landlord or the tenant may terminate a tenancy at will by giving one month's written notice before the next rent due date.

Georgia has NO just cause eviction requirement. No-fault eviction at end of lease term is permitted without cause.

Notice requirements:
- Nonpayment of rent: No specific statutory notice period; landlord may file dispossessory action immediately after rent is past due. In practice, written demand (3-7 days) is given.
- Month-to-month: 30-day written notice from either party.
- Fixed-term lease: No renewal; landlord need not give notice if lease expires per its terms (unless lease requires it).

Dispossessory Process (O.C.G.A. §§44-7-50 to 44-7-59): After filing, the court issues a summons to the tenant who has 7 days to answer. Trial is set within 30 days. If writ of possession granted, tenant has 7 days to appeal or vacate.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Georgia has NO specific statewide statute governing landlord entry and advance notice requirements for residential tenancies. Entry rights are governed by the lease agreement and common law.

Georgia courts generally hold that reasonable notice (24-48 hours) is required for non-emergency inspections and repairs. Emergency entry is permitted without notice.

O.C.G.A. § 44-7-13. LANDLORD'S DUTY TO KEEP PREMISES IN REPAIR. The landlord must keep the premises in repair, which includes the right to enter and make repairs.

Leases should specify entry notice requirements. If silent, Georgia courts may imply reasonable notice.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Georgia has NO statewide rent control and no local rent control in any municipality. No preemption statute exists because no locality has attempted rent control. Rent increases are unlimited and governed by lease terms or 30-day notice for month-to-month tenancies.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Georgia has no statewide statute capping late fees. Late fees must be: (1) stated in the lease; (2) reasonable. Georgia courts generally treat late fees as liquidated damages provisions, enforceable if they represent a reasonable estimate of actual loss.

Common practice: 5-10% of monthly rent after a 5-day grace period.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
O.C.G.A. § 44-7-3. DELIVERY OF WRITTEN STATEMENT OF DEFECTS. Landlord must provide tenant with a written list of existing defects or damages to the premises prior to or at commencement of tenancy.

Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.

Georgia Code § 44-7-20: Written lease requirement for residential rental of 12 months or more.

No specific mold, flood zone, or radon disclosure statute for landlord-tenant transactions in Georgia.

Property manager disclosure: O.C.G.A. §43-40-1 et seq.: Real estate agents acting as property managers must comply with licensing requirements.

other_provisions.domestic_violence: O.C.G.A. §44-7-22.1: A victim of family violence may terminate a residential lease early without penalty if a family violence protection order or police report is provided to the landlord. Tenant must provide 30 days written notice.
other_provisions.retaliation: Georgia has no specific anti-retaliation statute. Courts apply common law principles: eviction proceedings filed in retaliation for good-faith complaints may be defenses or counterclaims.
other_provisions.no_just_cause: Georgia does not require just cause for eviction at end of lease term. Landlord may choose not to renew for any reason.
other_provisions.self_help_eviction: Georgia prohibits self-help eviction (lockouts, utility shutoffs). Landlord must use judicial dispossessory process. Criminal liability possible for improper lockouts.
other_provisions.application_fees: No specific cap on application fees in Georgia.
other_provisions.early_termination: Governed by lease terms. Georgia follows contract law; early termination clauses enforceable if reasonable.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) Chapter 765: 765 ILCS 705/ (Landlord and Tenant Act); 765 ILCS 710/ (Security Deposit Return Act); 765 ILCS 715/ (Security Deposit Interest Act); 765 ILCS 720/ (Retaliatory Eviction Act); 765 ILCS 735/ (Rental Property Utility Service Act); 765 ILCS 742/ (Residential Tenants' Right to Repair Act). NOTE: Chicago has separate, more protective ordinance – Chicago Residential Landlord and Tenant Ordinance (RLTO), Municipal Code Chapter 5-12.
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (current through 2024 P.A.; Chicago RLTO frequently amended)
jurisdictions.notes: Illinois is a split-system state. Statewide statutes are relatively landlord-friendly. However, Chicago RLTO (and certain Cook County ordinances) provide extensive tenant protections comparable to NYC or California. Chicago: 30-day return of security deposit; interest required; just cause eviction for 2+ year tenants (2021); written move-in inventory; 48-hour entry notice.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
STATEWIDE: 765 ILCS 710/ – SECURITY DEPOSIT RETURN ACT.

Section 1: A landlord of residential real property, containing 5 or more units, who has received a security deposit from a tenant to secure the payment of rent or to compensate for damage to the leased property, shall return the deposit to the tenant within 30 days after the date that the tenant vacates the premises.

Section 2: If the landlord is entitled to withhold any portion of the deposit, the landlord shall, within 30 days after the date that the tenant vacates the premises, furnish to the tenant an itemized statement of the damage allegedly caused by the tenant and the estimated or actual cost for repairing or replacing each item on that statement, together with the amount of the security deposit being withheld. The itemized statement shall be sent by first class mail to the last known address of the tenant.

Section 3: If the landlord fails to return the security deposit or any part of it or fails to furnish the itemized statement within the 30-day period, the tenant may recover the security deposit, all interest due thereon, and damages in an amount equal to twice the amount of the security deposit, plus court costs and reasonable attorney's fees.

NOTE: For buildings with fewer than 5 units, the Security Deposit Return Act does not apply. The common law governs.

765 ILCS 715/ – SECURITY DEPOSIT INTEREST ACT.
Section 2: A landlord of residential property must pay interest on security deposits held for more than 6 months equal to the deposit account interest rate published by the Illinois Office of Banks and Real Estate. Interest must be paid annually (or upon return of deposit).
Exception: Does not apply to buildings with fewer than 25 units OR if the landlord owns fewer than 25 units total.

CHICAGO RLTO §5-12-080:
Deposit must be: (1) deposited in federally insured interest-bearing account; (2) receipt provided to tenant; (3) deposit and accrued interest returned within 30 days of move-out; (4) itemized statement of deductions within 30 days. Interest rate: Chicago published rate (typically 0.01% in recent years). Failure: Tenant may recover deposit + twice the deposit as damages + attorney's fees.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
765 ILCS 705/5 (General Rule): Not specific to residential entry; lease governs.

CHICAGO RLTO §5-12-050. LANDLORD'S RIGHT OF ACCESS.
(a) The landlord shall have the right of access to the dwelling unit at reasonable times with the consent of the tenant for the purpose of making inspections, alterations or repairs.
(b) EXCEPT IN EMERGENCY: The landlord must give the tenant at least 2 DAYS' notice of the landlord's intent to enter, and may enter only at reasonable times.
(c) If the tenant refuses consent after proper notice, the landlord may enter for emergency or court-ordered purposes only.
(d) The landlord shall not abuse the right of access or use it to harass the tenant.
(e) Tenants may change or add locks, provided they give landlord a key within 48 hours.

[For properties outside Chicago with no specific ordinance: Reasonable notice (24-48 hours) is industry standard; lease terms govern.]
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
765 ILCS 735/ – RENTAL PROPERTY UTILITY SERVICE ACT.
Landlord cannot cause termination of utility service to tenant (heat, running water, garbage collection, etc.) except in cases of emergency or for necessary repairs.

765 ILCS 742/ – RESIDENTIAL TENANTS' RIGHT TO REPAIR ACT.
If landlord fails to repair condition within 14 days of written notice that endangers health or safety, tenant in a building of 5 or more units may:
(1) Have the condition repaired; AND
(2) Deduct repair costs from rent (up to $500 or one month's rent, whichever is less).
Tenant must first give written notice to landlord of defects.

CHICAGO RLTO §5-12-110. WARRANTY OF HABITABILITY.
(a) A rental agreement shall be deemed to include a covenant of quiet enjoyment and a covenant of habitability, implying the landlord will maintain the premises in accordance with applicable building codes and in a condition fit for habitation.
(b) Tenant remedies for landlord breach:
(1) Terminate lease with 14 days notice if condition materially affects health/safety;
(2) Withhold rent in amount reflecting diminished value (must notify landlord and deposit withheld rent in escrow);
(3) Repair and deduct (up to $500 or one month's rent);
(4) Sue for actual damages.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section:
Illinois has NO statewide rent control. A 2022 effort to repeal the statewide preemption (which allows Chicago to enact rent control) passed the Illinois Senate but was not enacted into law. Currently, local governments in Illinois are PREEMPTED from enacting rent control under 310 ILCS 50/ – Rent Control Preemption Act (1997).

Note: As of 2025, discussions continue about allowing Chicago to enact rent stabilization; watch for future legislative developments.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Illinois has no statewide cap on late fees for residential tenancies (the Landlord-Tenant Act does not specify). Common practice: late fees must be stated in lease; 5% of monthly rent after 5-day grace period is typical.

CHICAGO RLTO §5-12-140(c): A landlord may not assess a late charge unless: (1) the lease specifies the amount; (2) the charge does not exceed $10 or 5% of the monthly rent, whichever is greater; and (3) rent has been late for more than 5 days.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
CHICAGO RLTO §5-12-100:
(1) Before accepting rental, landlord must disclose in writing: all material facts about the physical condition of the unit known to the landlord; required disclosures include: building and elevator code violations pending; litigation affecting the building; presence of toxic materials.
(2) Summary of Chicago RLTO must be provided to tenants in writing at time of lease signing.

Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.

765 ILCS 735/2 (Utility Service Disclosure): Landlord providing utility services must disclose charges and billing method.

Illinois radon awareness act (420 ILCS 46/): Landlord must provide EPA radon guide to tenants at commencement of tenancy.

other_provisions.chicago_rlto_summary: Chicago RLTO (Municipal Code §5-12) applies to all residential rental units in Chicago. Key provisions: 2-day entry notice; 30-day deposit return; interest on deposits; detailed move-in disclosure; just cause eviction for 2+ year tenants; relocation assistance for certain no-fault evictions; penalties for violations (2x deposit, actual damages, attorney's fees).
other_provisions.retaliatory_eviction: 765 ILCS 720/ – Retaliatory Eviction Act: A landlord may not terminate or refuse to renew a lease for the reason that the tenant has complained to any governmental authority of a bona fide violation of any applicable building code, housing code, or similar regulation.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: 765 ILCS 750/ – Safe Homes Act: Victims of domestic violence, sexual violence, or gender violence may terminate lease with 3-14 days notice (depending on circumstances) with documentation.
other_provisions.repair_and_deduct: 765 ILCS 742/: Statewide repair-and-deduct rights for buildings ≥5 units. Tenant may deduct up to $500 or 1 month's rent after proper notice and 14-day cure period.
other_provisions.application_fees: No statewide cap outside Chicago. Chicago RLTO §5-12-170: Application fees may not exceed the actual cost of processing the application.
other_provisions.chicago_relocation: Chicago RLTO §5-12-130(g): For no-fault evictions (demolition, renovation, removal from market, owner move-in), landlord must pay relocation assistance equal to: 1 month's rent (units <1 year); 2 months' rent (1-3 years); 3 months' rent (3+ years occupancy).
other_provisions.smoke_detectors: Illinois Smoke Detector Act (425 ILCS 60/): Landlord must install and maintain smoke detectors in each bedroom and common area.

jurisdictions.title: Indiana Residential Landlord-Tenant Law
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Ind. Code §§ 32-31-3-1 to 32-31-3-18

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline: 45 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written statement of deductions if any amount is withheld.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord must either (1) deposit in a federally insured financial institution in Indiana, or (2) post a surety bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Abandoned property: If tenant abandons the premises, landlord may apply security deposit to rent owed.
- Landlord's liability: If landlord fails to return the deposit within 45 days, tenant may sue for damages.
- No penalty provision: Indiana does not provide for statutory damages; tenant must prove actual damages.
Ind. Code § 32-31-3-1: Defines security deposit as any advance or deposit of money, regardless of form or description.
Ind. Code § 32-31-3-14: Requires return of security deposit within 45 days after termination of tenancy.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Ind. Code §§ 32-31-5-1 to 32-31-5-12

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through court.
- Nonpayment of rent notice: 10 days written notice required before filing eviction. If tenant pays all rent due within 10 days, tenancy is reinstated.
- Lease violation: Landlord may provide notice specifying the violation and giving tenant 10-30 days to cure, depending on lease terms.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT lock out tenants, remove belongings, or shut off utilities without a court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-5 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in small claims court (up to $6,000) or circuit/superior court; summons issued; hearing scheduled.
- Judgment: If landlord prevails, court enters judgment for possession and may include unpaid rent and court costs.
- Eviction for cause: Material noncompliance with lease; failure to pay rent; holding over after lease expiration.
Ind. Code § 32-31-5-1: Governs termination for nonpayment of rent. Landlord must give at least 10 days notice.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Ind. Code §§ 32-31-4-1 to 32-31-4-10

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Indiana landlords have an obligation to maintain fit and habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with housing codes; make repairs; keep premises safe and sanitary; provide functioning heating, plumbing, electrical; supply hot water.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: NOT ALLOWED under Indiana law.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant cannot unilaterally withhold rent.
  3. Court action: Tenant may sue to enforce landlord's repair obligations or seek damages.
  4. Code enforcement: Tenant may report violations to local building department.
  5. Termination: In extreme cases of uninhabitability, tenant may terminate the lease.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. Ind. Code § 32-31-9-5 prohibits landlords from evicting in retaliation for tenant's complaints to government agencies or exercise of rights.
- Rent control: None. Indiana has no statewide or local rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local and state building codes.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Ind. Code § 32-31-4-2

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum, but must be reasonable and proportionate to actual damages.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement/lease.
- Court may reduce late fees deemed excessive.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Ind. Code § 32-31-4-4

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Indiana law requires 'reasonable notice' but does not specify a minimum time period.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs or provide services.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show the premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Should be at reasonable times during normal business hours.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
Ind. Code § 32-31-4-4: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only: (1) at reasonable times; (2) in a reasonable manner; (3) after proper notice.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Ind. Code § 32-31-4-3

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Indiana.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice of a rent increase.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term unless the lease provides otherwise.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Renewal terms: When a lease renews automatically, landlord may change terms including rent.

jurisdictions.title: Kansas Residential Landlord-Tenant Law
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
K.S.A. § 58-2550

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount:
  * Unfurnished units: 1 month's rent
  * Furnished units: 1.5 months' rent
  * Pet deposit: An additional one-half month's rent may be charged for pets
- Return deadline: 30 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord must hold deposit in a banking institution in Kansas; must maintain as a separate account or post bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Mailing requirement: Landlord must mail the deposit and/or itemized deductions to tenant's last known address within 30 days.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return deposit within 30 days without legitimate deduction, tenant may recover up to twice the amount wrongfully withheld.
- Transfer of landlord's interest: New landlord must notify tenant of transfer within 30 days; new landlord becomes responsible for the deposit.
K.S.A. § 58-2550(2): 'The landlord shall return the security deposit within thirty days after the tenancy has terminated... If the landlord retains any portion of the security deposit, the landlord shall provide the tenant with a written statement itemizing the deductions.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
K.S.A. §§ 58-2562 to 58-2564

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent notice: 3 days notice to quit in most cases; some leases may require 10 days.
- Material noncompliance: 14 days notice to cure, followed by 14 days to vacate if not cured.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Holdover: After lease expiration, tenant becomes a month-to-month tenant and 30-day notice applies.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT use self-help measures; must obtain court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in district court; tenant is served with summons; hearing scheduled within days.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, material noncompliance, end of lease term, nuisance or illegal activity.
- Mitigation: Landlord has duty to mitigate damages by attempting to re-rent after early termination.
K.S.A. § 58-2564: If landlord fails to comply with the requirements for returning the security deposit, tenant may recover damages not exceeding the entire deposit.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
K.S.A. §§ 58-2553 to 58-2561

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: K.S.A. § 58-2553 imposes a duty on landlords to maintain fit and habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with applicable building and housing codes; make repairs to keep premises in good condition; maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: ALLOWED. K.S.A. § 58-2559: After giving landlord 14 days written notice and landlord fails to repair, tenant may have repairs made and deduct costs from rent.
  2. Rent withholding: ALLOWED. K.S.A. § 58-2560: After 14 days written notice, tenant may withhold rent if landlord fails to make necessary repairs.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair habitability.
  4. Action for damages: Tenant may bring action for damages and injunctive relief.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. K.S.A. § 58-2572 prohibits landlords from evicting in retaliation for tenant's complaints or exercise of rights.
- Rent control: None. Kansas has no statewide rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local housing codes.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
K.S.A. § 58-2550

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
K.S.A. § 58-2555

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Kansas law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum is stated in statute.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver items, or inspect the premises.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
- Court enforcement: Landlord may obtain a court order to gain entry if tenant unreasonably withholds consent.
K.S.A. § 58-2555: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only at reasonable times in a reasonable manner for the purpose of making repairs, delivering parcels, or exhibiting the dwelling unit to prospective tenants, purchasers, or workers.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
K.S.A. § 58-2552

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Kansas. State law does not prohibit local governments from enacting rent control, but no city has done so.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Renewal: Landlord may offer renewal at different terms including higher rent.

sources.name: Kansas Office of Revisor of Statutes

jurisdictions.title: Kentucky Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
KRS § 383.580

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline:
  * If tenant provides forwarding address: 30 days after tenancy ends
  * If tenant does NOT provide forwarding address: 60 days after tenancy ends (landlord must make reasonable efforts to locate tenant)
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord must hold security deposit in a banking institution in Kentucky, in an account separate from operating funds, or post a surety bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return deposit or provide itemized deductions within the required period, tenant may recover twice the amount wrongfully withheld.
- Transfer of ownership: New landlord is responsible for security deposits; must notify tenant within 30 days.
KRS § 383.580: 'The landlord shall return the security deposit within thirty days after termination of the tenancy and delivery of possession, less any lawful deductions.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
KRS §§ 383.655–383.715

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: 7 days written notice to quit. If tenant pays overdue rent within 7 days, tenancy is reinstated.
- Material noncompliance: 14 days written notice specifying violation, with 14 days right to cure.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Holdover: After lease expiration, tenant becomes a month-to-month tenant requiring 30-day notice.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT use self-help measures; must obtain court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-5 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in district court; summons served on tenant; hearing within days.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, material noncompliance, end of lease term, nuisance or illegal activity.
- Writ of possession: After judgment, sheriff enforces the removal of tenant.
KRS § 383.655: A landlord may bring an action for possession after proper notice has been given and the tenant has failed to comply.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
KRS §§ 383.595, 383.625–383.640

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: KRS § 383.595 imposes a duty on landlords to maintain fit and habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with applicable building and housing codes; keep premises in good repair; maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; provide garbage collection.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: ALLOWED. KRS § 383.625: After giving landlord written notice and reasonable time to repair, tenant may have repairs made and deduct costs from rent.
  2. Rent withholding: ALLOWED. KRS § 383.640: After giving landlord written notice and reasonable time to repair, tenant may withhold rent.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health or safety.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action for damages and injunctive relief.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. KRS § 383.705 prohibits landlords from evicting in retaliation for tenant's complaints or exercise of rights.
- Rent control: None. Kentucky has no statewide or local rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local housing codes.
KRS § 383.595: 'The landlord shall (1) comply with applicable building and housing codes; (2) make repairs to keep the premises fit and habitable; (3) maintain the dwelling unit in a clean and sanitary condition.'
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
KRS § 383.580

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
