- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
KRS § 383.610

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Kentucky law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum hours mandated by statute.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver packages, or inspect the premises.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times, generally during normal business hours.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
- Court order: If tenant unreasonably withholds consent, landlord may obtain a court order to gain entry.
KRS § 383.610: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only at reasonable times in a reasonable manner for the purpose of making repairs, providing services, delivering packages, or exhibiting the dwelling unit.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
KRS §§ 383.500–383.715

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Kentucky.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Landlord may raise rent by any amount with proper notice.

jurisdictions.title: Louisiana Residential Landlord and Tenant Law
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
La. R.S. § 9:3251–9:3254

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline: 30 days after lease ends and tenant vacates the premises.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions within 30 days.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Not specifically required by Louisiana statute.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return deposit or provide itemized deductions within 30 days, landlord may be liable for the entire deposit plus attorney's fees.
- Civil law system: Louisiana uses a civil law system; rental relationships are governed primarily by the Civil Code.
- Lease agreement controls: Louisiana law gives great weight to the parties' lease agreement.
La. R.S. § 9:3251: Requires landlords to return security deposits within 30 days after termination of the lease and delivery of possession.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
La. C.C.P. arts. 4701–4735; La. R.S. 13:4401–13:4406

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: Louisiana law does not require pre-litigation notice for nonpayment; landlord typically files directly for eviction. Some leases provide for a 5-day grace period.
- Month-to-month termination: 10 days written notice required (La. Civ. Code art. 2728). This is shorter than most states.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords must obtain court judgment before removing tenant.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files 'rule for possession' in justice of the peace court or city court; expedited process for nonpayment.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, breach of lease, end of lease term.
- Eviction via constable: After judgment, constable or sheriff enforces the writ of possession.
La. C.C.P. art. 4701: Governs the eviction process. Landlord must have a right to possession to maintain an eviction action.
La. C.C.P. art. 4731: After judgment, court issues writ of possession; enforcement by sheriff or constable.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
La. Civ. Code arts. 2671, 2693

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: La. Civ. Code art. 2671 imposes a duty on landlords to maintain the leased premises in good repair.
- Landlord's duty: Deliver the premises in good condition; make repairs during the lease; maintain premises suitable for its intended purpose.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: NOT ALLOWED under Louisiana law.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant cannot unilaterally withhold rent.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the lease if premises become unusable for their intended purpose.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action against landlord for breach of the landlord's obligations.
  5. Report to authorities: Tenant may report code violations to local authorities.
- Retaliatory eviction: NOT specifically prohibited by state statute in Louisiana.
- Rent control: None. Louisiana has no statewide or local rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local building and housing codes.
La. Civ. Code art. 2671: 'The lessor guarantees the lessee that the thing leased is suitable for its purpose and maintains that suitability throughout the lease.'
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
La. Civ. Code; La. R.S. 9:3251

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period; depends on lease terms.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
La. Civ. Code arts. 2680, 2681

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Louisiana civil code requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum hours mandated.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs or provide services.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants or buyers.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably refuse access.
La. Civ. Code art. 2680: The lessee may not prevent the lessor from entering the premises to make repairs or to exhibit the premises to prospective tenants or purchasers.
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
La. Civ. Code arts. 2668–2729

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Louisiana.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 10 days advance written notice before the rent due date.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Louisiana's civil law tradition gives great flexibility to parties in structuring lease terms.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 186, §§ 15–15B (Security Deposits and Tenant Remedies); M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A (Eviction); M.G.L. c. 186, § 14 (Implied Warranty of Habitability)
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (HB 4739 of 2024 made significant changes to security deposit rules; Boston local ordinances updated 2024)
jurisdictions.notes: Massachusetts has strong statewide tenant protections. The 2024 reform law (HB 4739) changed security deposit interest requirements and added protections. Boston, Cambridge, and Somerville have additional local rent control (stabilization) and just cause eviction rules. The statewide rent increase notice requirement is 30 days.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Massachusetts landlord-tenant law spans multiple chapters. Key provisions: M.G.L. c. 186 § 15B (security deposits, interest), c. 186 § 14 (implied warranty of habitability), c. 186 § 15 (landlord entry), c. 239 § 8A (eviction procedures), c. 187 (security deposit interest rates).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B. Security deposits; interest; termination; landlord's statement of condition.
(1)(a) Security deposit limitation. A landlord may not demand or receive from a tenant a security deposit in an amount exceeding the amount of the first month's rent. The total of all deposits and last month's rent may not exceed the amount of first month's rent plus an amount equal to one-half of the first month's rent.
(1)(b) Separate account; interest. The landlord shall deposit the security deposit in a Massachusetts banking institution in a separate, interest-bearing account in the tenant's name. The landlord shall pay the tenant interest on the security deposit, at the rate determined annually by the Massachusetts Commissioner of Banks (currently 5% per annum or the regular passbook rate, whichever is greater), in yearly installments, or at the termination of tenancy, whichever occurs first. The tenant is entitled to receive the interest earned while residing in the premises.
(2) Landlord's statement of condition. Within 10 days after the tenant commences occupation of the premises, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant a written statement of the condition of the premises. The tenant may supplement or dispute the statement.
(3) Return of security deposit. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions, within 30 days after the tenant vacates the premises. The landlord shall provide an itemized list of any deductions with supporting receipts.
(4) Failure to comply. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 30 days, the tenant may bring an action in the district court or housing court to recover the security deposit and may be awarded damages equal to three times the amount of the security deposit, plus interest and reasonable attorney's fees.
(7) Retaliation. The taking of a security deposit shall not be construed as a waiver of any other rights of the landlord or tenant.

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 14-day notice to quit for nonpayment (c. 239, § 8A); summary process action follows
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to quit for month-to-month tenancy (or longer if lease specifies); 7-day notice for week-to-week tenancy
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 30-day notice to cure or quit for material breach
notice_requirements.no_cause: 30-day notice to terminate month-to-month tenancy (statewide); Boston requires just cause for eviction of covered units

eviction_section.full_text:
M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A. Summary process for nonpayment of rent. (a) A landlord may bring a summary process action for nonpayment of rent if the rent is 7 days or more overdue. The landlord must first give the tenant a 14-day notice to quit. (b) The court shall grant judgment for the landlord if the tenant fails to pay all overdue rent and reasonable costs within 7 days after the return day. (c) If the tenant pays all overdue rent and costs before judgment, the action shall be dismissed.
M.G.L. c. 186, § 18. Retaliatory eviction. No landlord may evict or attempt to evict a tenant solely because the tenant has exercised any right under this chapter or any other law. If the landlord brings an eviction action within 6 months of the tenant's protected activity, there is a rebuttable presumption of retaliation.
Boston Just Cause Eviction (Local Ordinance, effective 2024): Covered residential units may not be evicted except for just cause, which includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, and substantial rehabilitation.

landlord_entry_section.full_text: M.G.L. c. 186, § 15. Right of entry. A landlord may enter a tenant's premises: (1) At reasonable times upon reasonable notice (generally accepted as 24 hours); (2) In case of emergency without notice; (3) To make repairs or improvements; (4) To show the premises to prospective tenants or buyers. The tenant may recover damages for unlawful entry or harassment. Entry without proper notice may constitute a violation of the tenant's rights.

habitability_section.full_text: M.G.L. c. 186, § 14. Implied warranty of fitness for habitation. In every written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This means the premises must be suitable for occupation in the area where they are located and must include: (1) Effective waterproofing of the roof and exterior walls; (2) Plumbing and gas facilities in good working order; (3) Clean and sanitary conditions; (4) Functioning heating facilities adequate to heat all habitable rooms; (5) Electrical lighting and outlets in good working order; (6) Freedom from vermin and pests; (7) Compliance with state sanitary codes and local health ordinances. This warranty may not be waived in a residential tenancy. If the landlord violates this warranty, the tenant may: (a) Withhold rent; (b) Bring an action for damages; (c) Terminate the lease; (d) In Boston, Cambridge, and some other municipalities, repair and deduct.

rent_control_section.summary: Massachusetts has no statewide rent control, but local municipalities may adopt rent stabilization ordinances. Boston has a rent stabilization ordinance (effective since 2016, amended 2024) covering most residential units. It limits annual rent increases to no more than 10% plus inflation (CPI), with exemptions for new construction and owner-occupied units. Cambridge has its own rent control ordinance. Statewide rent increase notice: 30 days.
rent_control_section.statutes: Boston Municipal Code, Chapter XVI; Cambridge City Code

late_fees_section.full_text: Massachusetts courts have held that late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written rental agreement. There is no statutory cap on late fees, but excessive late fees may be reduced or voided as penalties under common law. Reasonable late fees typically do not exceed 5% of monthly rent or a fixed dollar amount (e.g., $20-$50). Boston local rules may impose additional requirements.

key_disclosures: M.G.L. c. 186 § 15B: Landlord must provide statement of premises condition within 10 days of tenant's occupation.
M.G.L. c. 186 § 15B: Landlord must disclose the location of the account where the security deposit is held.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law (42 U.S.C. § 4852d) for pre-1978 housing.
Boston: Landlord must register the rental unit with the Boston Inspectional Services Department.
Boston: Must disclose any pending code violations.

other_provisions.retaliation: M.G.L. c. 186, § 18: Retaliatory eviction is prohibited. Rebuttable presumption if landlord acts within 6 months of tenant's protected activity.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: M.G.L. c. 186, § 20: Domestic violence victims may request lock changes and may terminate the lease with 30 days notice and documentation.
other_provisions.security_deposit_interest: M.G.L. c. 187: Interest rate on security deposits is set annually by the Commissioner of Banks (currently approximately 5% or the regular passbook rate, whichever is higher).

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Maryland Real Property Code, Title 8 – Landlord and Tenant
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (Maryland General Assembly 2025 session; HB 18 and HB 269 enacted 2025)
jurisdictions.notes: Maryland enacted significant tenant protections in 2025 (HB 18 - Just Cause Eviction for certain counties, HB 269 - security deposit interest). Prince George's County, Montgomery County, and Baltimore City have additional local protections. The statewide Just Cause Eviction law (HB 18, 2025) requires just cause for eviction in most counties starting January 2026.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Maryland's landlord-tenant law is codified in Real Property Article, Title 8. Key sections include: § 8-203 (security deposit), § 8-207 (landlord duties, mitigation), § 8-211 (rent escrow), § 8-401 (nonpayment eviction), § 8-402 (lease violation eviction), § 8-402.1 (holdover), § 8-216 (landlord remedies).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
Md. Real Property Code § 8-203. Security deposit.
(b) Maximum amount. A landlord may not charge or collect a security deposit in an aggregate amount that exceeds the equivalent of 2 months' rent. However, a landlord may collect, at the time of the initial rental period, a security deposit in an amount not exceeding the equivalent of 3 months' rent if the landlord informs the tenant, in writing, that the tenant is entitled to pay the security deposit in 2 monthly installments.
(e)(1) Return deadline. Within 45 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions, together with an itemized accounting of the deductions. (2) If the landlord makes a deduction, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant by first-class mail addressed to the tenant's last known address a written itemized list of the deductions together with a statement of the basis for and amount of any deductions and a refund of the security deposit. (3) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 45 days, the tenant may bring an action for damages for failure to return the security deposit.
(g)(1) Interest. A landlord shall pay interest on any security deposit held in a fiduciary capacity for a tenant at a rate equal to the greater of: (i) The rate paid on standard passbook savings accounts as set by the Federal Reserve; or (ii) One percent. (2) The interest shall be paid to the tenant within 30 days after the anniversary date of the tenancy and within 30 days after termination of the tenancy. (HB 269 of 2025 made changes to interest payment requirements.)

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: Notice to pay or quit varies by county; typically 5-day notice to pay or vacate under § 8-401
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 1-month notice to quit for month-to-month (RC 8-402.1); 1-week notice for week-to-week tenancy
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 30-day notice to cure for material breach (RC 8-402)
notice_requirements.just_cause_required: HB 18 (2025) requires just cause for eviction starting January 2026 in most counties

eviction_section.full_text:
Md. Real Property Code § 8-401. Failure to pay rent. (a) If rent is due and unpaid, the landlord may terminate the tenancy and recover possession of the premises in the same manner as if the tenancy was for a definite term but was created orally or in writing. (b) The landlord may give the tenant written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises within the time provided in the lease, or if no time is provided, within a reasonable time. (c) If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may bring an action for possession and rent.
Md. Real Property Code § 8-402. Breach of lease covenant. If the tenant breaches a covenant or condition of the lease, the landlord may give the tenant written notice specifying the breach and stating that the lease will terminate upon a date not less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied. If the breach is remediable and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within the notice period, the lease does not terminate.
Md. Real Property Code § 8-402.1. Holding over after expiration of term. If the tenant holds over after the expiration of the lease term, the landlord may bring an action for possession. For month-to-month tenancies, at least one month's notice to quit is required. For week-to-week tenancies, at least one week's notice to quit is required.

landlord_entry_section.statutes: Md. Code, Real Property § 8-208 (implied); common law
landlord_entry_section.full_text: Maryland does not have a comprehensive statutory provision governing landlord entry. Under common law, a landlord may enter a rental premises only: (1) At reasonable times; (2) Upon reasonable notice (typically 24 hours is considered reasonable); (3) For legitimate purposes such as repairs, inspections, or showing the property to prospective tenants or buyers; (4) In emergencies without notice. The tenant may recover damages for unlawful entry or harassment. There is no specific statutory notice period, but 24-hour advance notice is generally accepted as reasonable practice.

habitability_section.full_text:
Md. Real Property Code § 8-211. Landlord's failure to comply with lease; rent escrow. (a) If the landlord fails to comply with the landlord's obligations under the lease, the tenant may: (1) Bring an action for damages; (2) Withhold rent and deposit rent into court if the premises are not fit and habitable; (3) Terminate the lease if the landlord's noncompliance is material. (b) If the tenant deposits rent into court, the tenant shall continue to deposit rent as it becomes due throughout the court proceedings. (c) The tenant may recover damages for the landlord's failure to maintain the premises in a fit and habitable condition.
Md. Real Property Code § 8-207. Mitigation of damages. (a) The landlord shall make reasonable efforts to mitigate damages caused by the tenant's breach of the lease. (b) The landlord shall mitigate damages by attempting to rent the premises to another tenant at a reasonable rent.

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Maryland has no statewide rent control. Montgomery County and Prince George's County have rent stabilization programs. Baltimore City has rent range regulations. The statewide Just Cause Eviction law (HB 18, 2025) takes effect January 2026.

late_fees_section.full_text: Maryland courts have held that late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written lease. There is no statutory cap on late fees, but courts may reduce or void excessive late fees. A late fee that exceeds the actual damages caused by late payment may be deemed a penalty and unenforceable. Maryland follows the common law rule that late fees must bear a reasonable relationship to anticipated damages.

key_disclosures: § 8-203: Security deposit must be deposited in a Maryland financial institution, and landlord must disclose the location of the account.
§ 8-213: Landlord must provide a copy of the lease and any rules and regulations to the tenant.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law for pre-1978 housing.
§ 8-601 et seq.: Maryland Transit-Oriented Development disclosure requirements for certain properties near transit.

other_provisions.just_cause_eviction: HB 18 (2025): Starting January 2026, landlords must have just cause to terminate tenancies in most Maryland counties. Just cause includes nonpayment of rent, lease violations, nuisance, and other specified grounds.
other_provisions.retaliation: Md. Real Property Code § 8-216: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights. Presumption of retaliation applies if adverse action taken within 60 days of tenant's protected activity.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: Md. Code, Courts § 4-505: Domestic violence victims may request a name change on lease and protective orders affecting tenancy.
other_provisions.mitigation: § 8-207: Landlord has duty to mitigate damages from tenant breach by attempting to re-rent at reasonable rent.

security_deposit.max_amount: Generally capped at 1 month's rent; limited exceptions can allow up to 2 months (confirm the exception criteria before charging more).

allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Other charges authorized by lease or law

lease_termination.early_termination_by_tenant: Domestic violence/sexual assault/stalking victims (with documentation); SCRA for military
lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Michigan Compiled Laws §§ 554.601-554.641 (Landlord and Tenant Relationships Act); MCL 554.631-554.639 (Security Deposit Act)
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (Public Act 150 of 2024 made changes to security deposit procedures and eviction timelines)
jurisdictions.notes: Michigan is a landlord-friendly state with no statewide rent control or just cause eviction requirement. Detroit has local tenant protections including just cause eviction ordinance (effective 2021) and relocation assistance requirements. The Security Deposit Act (MCL 554.631 et seq.) sets strict requirements with severe penalties for non-compliance.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Michigan landlord-tenant law is governed primarily by the Landlord and Tenant Relationships Act (MCL 554.601 et seq.) and the Security Deposit Act (MCL 554.631 et seq.). Key sections: MCL 554.602 (security deposit maximum), MCL 554.603 (landlord duties), MCL 554.604 (tenant duties), MCL 554.605 (rental agreement), MCL 554.631-554.639 (security deposit requirements, return, penalties).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
MCL 554.631. Security deposit act; definitions. As used in this act: Security deposit means a deposit, including a last month's rent deposit, of money, the purpose of which is to secure the performance of a tenant under a lease or rental agreement for a dwelling unit. Landlord means the owner, lessor, or sublessor of a rental unit or the property of which it is a part. Tenant means a person who is a lessee in possession of a dwelling unit.
MCL 554.632. Security deposit; maximum amount. A landlord shall not require a security deposit in an amount exceeding an amount equal to 1-1/2 months' rent. A landlord shall not require a tenant to pay a security deposit in the form of cash, money order, or a check, or require the payment of money other than as described in this section.
MCL 554.633. Security deposit; written list of condition. Within 14 days after a tenant takes possession of a dwelling unit, the landlord shall provide the tenant with a written list of the condition of the premises. The tenant may add to or dispute the list.
MCL 554.634. Security deposit; return; itemized statement of damages. Within 30 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any lawful deductions, together with a written itemized statement of the basis for and amount of any deductions.
MCL 554.635. Security deposit; withholdings for damages; condition report. A landlord may withhold from the security deposit amounts reasonably necessary to pay for: (a) Physical damages to the premises caused by the tenant, excluding ordinary wear and tear; (b) Unpaid rent if itemized in a judgment or settlement agreement.
MCL 554.636. Failure to return deposit; penalty. If a landlord fails to return the security deposit within 30 days, the tenant may recover twice the amount of the security deposit as a civil penalty, together with actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.
MCL 554.639. Security deposit; interest; statement of escrow account. A landlord who holds a security deposit in excess of $100 for more than 6 months shall pay the tenant interest at the rate of 1% per annum or the rate determined by the Commissioner of Commerce, whichever is greater. The landlord shall maintain the security deposit in a banking institution in Michigan.

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 7-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (MCL 600.5750)
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy (MCL 554.134)
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 7-day notice for noncompliance with material lease terms; 30-day notice for repeated violations
notice_requirements.no_cause: No statewide just cause requirement; Detroit local ordinance requires just cause for covered units

eviction_section.full_text:
MCL 554.134. Termination of tenancy. A tenancy from year to year shall be terminated by a notice of 1 year to quit, given not less than 3 months before the end of the year. A tenancy from month to month shall be terminated by a notice of 1 month to quit, given not less than 1 month before the date on which the tenancy is to terminate.
MCL 600.5750. Summary proceeding; residential premises. (1) A landlord may bring a summary proceeding to recover possession of premises if the tenant fails to pay rent when due and has not paid within 7 days after the landlord gives written notice requiring the payment of rent or possession of the premises. (2) The summons shall be made returnable not less than 7 nor more than 10 days after service.
MCL 600.5752. Judgment for landlord. If the court finds that rent is due and unpaid, or that the tenant has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue a judgment for possession and restitution of the premises.

landlord_entry_section.full_text: Michigan law does not have a comprehensive statutory provision governing landlord entry. Under common law and court decisions, a landlord may enter the tenant's premises: (1) At reasonable times; (2) Upon reasonable notice (generally accepted as 24 hours); (3) For legitimate purposes such as repairs, inspections, or showing to prospective tenants or buyers; (4) In emergencies without notice. The tenant may recover damages for unlawful entry or harassment. MCL 554.603 requires the landlord to maintain the premises in a fit and habitable condition, which may necessitate entry with proper notice.

habitability_section.full_text:
MCL 554.603. Landlord's obligations. The landlord shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing code requirements that materially affect health and safety; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas in a safe and sanitary condition; (d) Maintain all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances in good working order; (e) Provide running water and reasonable hot water and heat unless the building is not required by law to be equipped for that purpose or the utilities are under the tenant's exclusive control.
MCL 554.604. Tenant's obligations. The tenant shall: (a) Comply with all applicable housing codes that materially affect health and safety; (b) Keep the premises safe and sanitary; (c) Dispose of garbage properly; (d) Not deliberately or negligently destroy, deface, damage, or remove any part of the premises; (e) Not engage in or permit drug-related criminal activity.
If the landlord fails to maintain fit and habitable premises, the tenant may: (a) Bring an action for damages; (b) Withhold rent and bring an action for rent overpayment; (c) Terminate the lease; (d) Make repairs and deduct the cost from rent (limited to the lesser of repair cost or one month rent).

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Michigan has no statewide rent control. Local governments are generally prohibited from imposing rent control under MCL 123.411 et seq. Detroit has no rent control ordinance.

late_fees_section.full_text: MCL 554.605. Rental agreement. The landlord and tenant may include any terms and conditions in the rental agreement that are not inconsistent with the act or other law. Late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written rental agreement. Michigan courts have held that late fees must bear a reasonable relationship to actual damages and may be reduced or voided if deemed a penalty. There is no statutory cap on late fees.

key_disclosures: MCL 554.633: Landlord must provide written list of premises condition within 14 days of tenant taking possession.
MCL 554.639: Landlord must disclose the banking institution where the security deposit is held if over $100.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law for pre-1978 housing.
Detroit local ordinance: Landlord must register rental properties with the City.

other_provisions.retaliation: MCL 554.606: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights under this act. If landlord takes adverse action within 6 months of tenant's protected activity, presumption of retaliation applies.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: Michigan does not have a statewide domestic violence lease termination statute, but courts have recognized certain defenses for domestic violence victims.
other_provisions.Detroit_just_cause: Detroit Ordinance Chapter 22, Article IV requires just cause for eviction of covered residential units and requires landlords to provide relocation assistance in certain circumstances.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Minnesota Statutes Chapter 504B – Residential Landlord and Tenant
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (HF 2 of 2023 (Foreclosure Eviction Reform), HF 4770 of 2024 made changes to eviction procedures and security deposits; Minneapolis/St. Paul have extensive local tenant protections)
jurisdictions.notes: Minnesota has strong statewide tenant protections. Minneapolis and St. Paul have comprehensive rent control ordinances (effective 2021) and just cause eviction requirements. The statewide 3-day eviction notice for nonpayment was extended to 14 days by HF 4770 (2024). First-generation Security Deposit Act (Chapter 504A) was replaced by Chapter 504B effective August 1, 2010.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Minnesota Statutes Chapter 504B governs residential tenancies. Key sections: 504B.125 (definitions), 504B.128 (rental agreement), 504B.161 (landlord duties), 504B.171 (tenant duties), 504B.178 (security deposit), 504B.211 (landlord entry), 504B.221 (warranty of habitability), 504B.331 (eviction notice), 504B.335 (eviction actions), 504B.381 (rent increase notice).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
Minn. Stat. § 504B.178. Security deposit.
Subdivision 1. Maximum amount. A landlord may not charge a security deposit that exceeds an amount equal to 2 months' rent. A landlord may permit a tenant to pay the security deposit in installments.
Subdivision 2. Receipt. The landlord shall give the tenant a written receipt for any security deposit.
Subdivision 3. Condition list. The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written inventory of the condition of the premises, including all furnishings and appliances, within 5 days after the tenant takes possession. The tenant may add to or dispute the inventory.
Subdivision 4. Interest. The landlord shall pay interest on the security deposit at the rate of 1% per year or at the rate determined by the Commissioner of Commerce, whichever is greater. The interest is payable to the tenant within 21 days after the end of each year of the tenancy.
Subdivision 5. Return of deposit. Within 21 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any lawful deductions. The landlord shall provide an itemized statement of any deductions with a list of the deductions and the balance due.
Subdivision 6. Penalties. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 21 days, the landlord is liable to the tenant for: (a) The full amount of the security deposit; (b) Treble damages if the failure was willful and malicious; (c) Reasonable attorney fees.

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 14-day notice to pay or vacate (HF 4770 of 2024 extended from 3 to 14 days effective 2024)
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 3-month notice to terminate for year-to-year tenancy; 1-month notice for month-to-month
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance
notice_requirements.no_cause: Statewide just cause not required; Minneapolis and St. Paul require just cause for covered units

eviction_section.full_text:
Minn. Stat. § 504B.331. Notice to vacate. (a) A landlord may bring an eviction action if the tenant fails to pay rent when due. The landlord shall give the tenant 14 days' written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises. (b) If the tenant fails to pay rent within the 14-day period, the landlord may bring an eviction action.
Minn. Stat. § 504B.335. Eviction action. The landlord shall bring an eviction action in conciliation court or district court. The court shall hold a hearing within 7 to 10 days after the complaint is served. If the court finds that the tenant owes rent or has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue an order for restitution of the premises.
Minneapolis Just Cause Eviction (Minneapolis Code of Ordinances, Title 16, Chapter 244): Landlords must have just cause to terminate a tenancy for covered units. Just cause includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, demolition, and substantial rehabilitation.
St. Paul Just Cause Eviction (St. Paul Legislative Code, Chapter 183): Similar just cause requirements apply to covered residential units in St. Paul.

landlord_entry_section.full_text:
Minn. Stat. § 504B.211. Landlord's right to enter the premises.
Subdivision 1. Lawful entry. The landlord may enter the premises only: (a) At a reasonable time; (b) In a reasonable manner; (c) After making a reasonable effort to notify the tenant of the intended entry. Reasonable notice is presumed to be at least 24 hours before the entry.
Subdivision 2. Permissible purposes. The landlord may enter the premises for the following purposes: (a) To inspect the premises; (b) To make ordinary, necessary, or agreed repairs, alterations, or improvements; (c) To supply necessary or agreed services; (d) To exhibit the premises to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors; (e) If the tenant is absent and the landlord reasonably believes that entry is necessary to protect the premises or property.
Subdivision 3. Emergency. The landlord may enter without notice in an emergency.
Subdivision 4. Tenant remedies. If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or makes a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, the tenant may recover actual damages or $500, whichever is greater, plus reasonable attorney fees.

habitability_section.full_text:
Minn. Stat. § 504B.161. Landlord's duties. The landlord shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing code requirements that materially affect health and safety; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas in a safe and sanitary condition; (d) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances; (e) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal if the landlord is responsible for removal; (f) Supply running water and reasonable amounts of hot water at all times and reasonable heat unless the heat is generated by a system under the tenant's exclusive control.
Minn. Stat. § 504B.221. Implied warranty of habitability. In every residential tenancy, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived. If the landlord violates this warranty, the tenant may: (a) Give written notice specifying the violation; (b) If the landlord fails to remedy within a reasonable time, the tenant may: (i) Withhold rent and bring a proceeding to determine the reasonable rental value; (ii) Recover damages; (iii) Terminate the lease; (iv) In Minneapolis and St. Paul, repair and deduct.

rent_control_section.summary: Minnesota has no statewide rent control, but Minneapolis (Ordinance 2021-031) and St. Paul (Ordinance 2021-010) enacted rent stabilization ordinances effective 2021. Minneapolis limits annual rent increases to 3% plus inflation (CPI), capped at 10% per year. St. Paul limits increases to 3% per year. Both cities have exemptions for new construction, owner-occupied units with fewer than 4 units, and subsidized housing. Statewide rent increase notice: 3 months for increases over 10%.
rent_control_section.statutes: Minneapolis Code of Ordinances Title 16, Chapter 244; St. Paul Legislative Code Chapter 183

late_fees_section.full_text: Minn. Stat. § 504B.128. Rental agreement. The landlord and tenant may include any terms and conditions not prohibited by Chapter 504B or other law. Late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written rental agreement. Minnesota courts have held that late fees must be a reasonable estimate of the landlord's actual damages. There is no statutory cap on late fees, but courts may void excessive fees. Minneapolis has additional restrictions on late fees for certain units.

key_disclosures: § 504B.178: Landlord must provide condition inventory within 5 days of tenant taking possession.
§ 504B.275: Landlord must disclose the name and address of the landlord or the person authorized to receive rent and notices.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law for pre-1978 housing.
Minneapolis: Landlord must provide written notice of the city's rental license number and contact information.
St. Paul: Landlord must register rental properties with the city.

other_provisions.retaliation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.441: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights. If landlord takes adverse action within 90 days of tenant's protected activity, presumption of retaliation applies.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: Minn. Stat. § 504B.206: Domestic violence victims may request lock changes and may terminate the lease with 30 days notice and documentation. Landlords may not penalize tenants for domestic violence.
other_provisions.foreclosure_protection: HF 2 of 2023: After foreclosure, tenants are entitled to at least 90 days' notice before being required to vacate.
other_provisions.rent_increase_notice: Minn. Stat. § 504B.381: Landlord must give at least 3 months' written notice for rent increases of more than 10%.

jurisdictions.title: Missouri Residential Landlord-Tenant Law
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 535.300

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: 2 months' rent (for unfurnished residential property).
- Return deadline: 30 days after tenancy ends AND after tenant provides forwarding address.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized statement of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear.
- Separate account: Not required by state law.
- Tenant's obligation: Tenant must provide forwarding address to the landlord.
- Landlord's obligation: Within 30 days after receiving tenant's forwarding address, return the deposit minus lawful deductions.
- If landlord fails to return deposit: Tenant may sue for actual damages plus attorney's fees.
- No security deposit interest requirement.
- Pet deposits: Allowed in addition to the security deposit.
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 535.300(1): 'The landlord shall deliver to the tenant within thirty days after the tenancy has terminated and the tenant has vacated the premises, the full security deposit or an itemized listing of the deductions from the security deposit and the remainder of the security deposit.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. §§ 441.510–441.880

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: Most jurisdictions do NOT require pre-litigation notice — landlord may file immediately. Some require a 5-day notice to quit.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Lease violation: Landlord must give tenant notice and opportunity to cure. Time period depends on the nature of the violation.
- Holdover: If tenant stays after lease expires, landlord may treat as month-to-month and must give 30-day notice.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Lockouts, utility shutoffs, and physical removal of tenant's belongings are illegal.
- Eviction timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
- Court procedure: Landlord files suit in associate circuit court; tenant is served; court hearing scheduled within days.
- Judgment: If landlord wins, court issues writ of execution; sheriff enforces removal.
- Types: Rent and possession, lease violation, end of lease term, nuisance.
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.740: Unlawful self-help evictions by landlords are prohibited. Tenant may sue for actual damages.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.234; § 441.500 et seq.

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Missouri recognizes that landlords must maintain rental premises in a fit and habitable condition.
- Landlord's duty: Maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; comply with housing codes.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: ALLOWED. Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.234: Tenant may have repairs made and deduct up to the lesser of $300 or one-half month's rent, per repair occurrence, after giving landlord proper written notice and reasonable time to repair.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant may NOT withhold rent for habitability issues.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health or safety.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.500 et seq. prohibits eviction in retaliation for tenant's good-faith complaints to government agencies.
- Rent control: No statewide rent control. Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.043 explicitly prohibits municipalities from enacting rent control ordinances.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable municipal building and housing codes.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 535.300; § 441.065

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum, but must be reasonable.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the lease.
- Missouri law allows landlords to charge reasonable late fees as specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.220

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Missouri law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. While no specific number of hours is mandated, courts generally recognize 24 hours as reasonable.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, inspections, or to supply necessary services.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show the premises to prospective tenants during the last 30 days of tenancy.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without prior notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times during normal business hours.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may change locks but must provide landlord a key upon request.
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.220: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only: (1) At reasonable times; (2) In a reasonable manner; (3) After proper notice to the tenant.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.043

Key provisions:
- Rent control: PROHIBITED statewide. Mo. Rev. Stat. § 441.043 expressly prohibits any Missouri municipality from enacting rent or price control ordinances.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Local ordinances: St. Louis and Kansas City have some additional tenant protections but cannot enact rent control.

jurisdictions.title: Mississippi Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-21

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline: 45 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord is required to hold security deposit in a separate account in a Mississippi banking institution, or post a surety bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return deposit within 45 days without legitimate deductions, tenant may recover actual damages, costs, and attorney's fees.
- Abandonment: Landlord may retain deposit to cover unpaid rent after tenant abandonment.
- No cap means landlords can charge any market-rate amount.
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-21: 'The landlord shall return the security deposit within forty-five (45) days after termination of the tenancy, less any deductions.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Miss. Code Ann. §§ 89-7-27 et seq.; § 89-8-13

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: 3 days written notice to quit. If tenant pays overdue rent plus any costs before judgment, tenancy may be reinstated.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Lease violation: Generally handled per lease terms; courts enforce lease provisions.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT use self-help methods; must obtain court judgment and writ of possession.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 1-4 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in justice court (for amounts typically up to $3,500) or county court; tenant served; hearing within days.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, breach of lease, holding over.
- Failure to pay: Court enters judgment for possession and unpaid rent.
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-7-27: Governs unlawful detainer and eviction procedures in justice court.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Miss. Code Ann. §§ 89-8-11 to 89-8-25

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Mississippi law recognizes landlord obligations to maintain habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with applicable building and housing codes; make repairs to keep premises in good condition; maintain structural, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: NOT ALLOWED under Mississippi law.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant cannot unilaterally withhold rent.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health or safety and landlord fails to repair after notice.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action against landlord for breach of duty.
- Retaliatory eviction: NOT specifically prohibited by state statute in Mississippi.
- Rent control: None. Mississippi has no statewide or local rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local housing codes.
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-25: Imposes duties on landlords to maintain fit and habitable premises.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-21

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-25

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Mississippi law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum hours mandated.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver items, or inspect the premises.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
Miss. Code Ann. § 89-8-25: Governs landlord's right of entry and tenant's obligation to permit entry for lawful purposes.
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Miss. Code Ann. §§ 89-8-1 to 89-8-29

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Mississippi.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Landlord mitigation: No statutory duty on landlord to mitigate damages from early lease termination.

security_deposit.max_amount: No statutory limit (custom and case law typically limit to 1-2 months' rent)
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Cleaning to bring unit to original move-in condition

tenant_remedies.rent_reduction: Contact local code enforcement or file lawsuit

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: North Carolina General Statutes (N.C.G.S.) Chapter 42 – Landlord and Tenant. Key Articles: Article 5 (§§42-38 to 42-44) – Residential Rental Agreements Act; Article 6 (§§42-50 to 42-56) – Tenant Security Deposit Act; Article 2A (§§42-25.6 to 42-25.9) – Expedited Ejectment; Article 3 (§§42-26 to 42-36.2) – Summary Ejectment
jurisdictions.notes: North Carolina is a moderately landlord-friendly state. No statewide rent control. Security deposit capped at 1.5 months (month-to-month) or 2 months (fixed term). 10-day notice for nonpayment. No just cause requirement. New protections added for domestic violence victims.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
N.C.G.S. § 42-26. TENANT'S BREACH; TERMINATION BY LANDLORD.
N.C.G.S. § 42-33. SERVICE OF NOTICE.
N.C.G.S. Article 3: SUMMARY EJECTMENT.

Notice requirements:
(1) Nonpayment of rent: 10-day demand for payment (N.C.G.S. §42-3). Tenant has 10 days from receipt to pay all past due rent before landlord can file for summary ejectment.
(2) Lease violation: 30-day written notice to cure or vacate (for most violations).
(3) Month-to-month termination: 7-day notice prior to end of rental period.
(4) Week-to-week: 2-day written notice.
(5) Year-to-year lease: No automatic renewal; typically ends at natural expiration.

N.C.G.S. § 42-25.9. TENANT'S REMEDIES FOR LANDLORD'S FAILURE TO COMPLY WITH ARTICLE.
Self-help eviction prohibited. Wrongful eviction: tenant may recover actual damages, or punitive damages.

Summary Ejectment Process (Article 3): Landlord files complaint in district court. Magistrate hearing typically within 7-10 days. If judgment for landlord, writ of possession issued 5 days after judgment. Tenant may appeal to district court.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
N.C.G.S. § 42-43(a)(8). Tenant obligations include: shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter the unit to inspect, make repairs, and show the unit.

North Carolina does NOT have a specific statutory advance notice requirement for landlord entry in the residential landlord-tenant statutes. Entry requirements are generally governed by lease provisions and common law.

Most leases and courts apply a standard of 24 hours advance notice for non-emergency inspections and repairs. Emergency entry permitted without notice. Self-help lockouts prohibited.

N.C.G.S. § 42-25.9(c): Landlord who wrongfully excludes tenant is liable for actual damages, or the difference between fair rental value and rent paid, whichever is greater, plus attorney's fees.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: North Carolina has NO statewide rent control and prohibits local rent control: N.C.G.S. §42-14.1: No city, county, or other governmental unit shall enact, maintain, or enforce any ordinance or resolution which would regulate or control the amount of rent charged for privately owned, single-family or multiple family residential rental property.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
N.C.G.S. § 42-46. LATE FEES AND OTHER FEES.
(a) A landlord may charge a late fee of the greater of: $15 or 5% of the monthly rent, but may not charge more than one late fee per rent payment.
(b) Grace period: Landlord may not charge a late fee until rent has been due for more than 5 days.
(c) Administrative fee for returned/NSF check: not to exceed $35.
(d) Late fees must be stated in the lease agreement.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
N.C.G.S. § 42-42.1: Landlord must provide tenant with a copy of the lease agreement.
N.C.G.S. § 42-42(a)(5): Landlord must provide operable smoke detectors.
Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.

North Carolina Residential Rental Agreements Act (§42-38) requires landlord to identify in writing: name and address of owner, and name and address of property manager if applicable.

Mold: No specific landlord-tenant disclosure statute; covered under general habitability.
Flood zone: No specific landlord-tenant statute (FHA regulations apply if applicable).

other_provisions.domestic_violence: N.C.G.S. §42-45.1: A tenant who is a victim of domestic violence, sexual offense, or stalking may terminate a rental agreement with 30 days written notice upon providing a copy of a valid protective order, police report, safety plan, etc.
other_provisions.military: N.C.G.S. §42-45: Active duty military may terminate lease with 30 days written notice.
other_provisions.retaliation: N.C.G.S. §42-37.1: Anti-retaliation. Landlord may not evict, raise rent, or reduce services because tenant made good-faith complaint to government agency about housing conditions. Rebuttable presumption of retaliation for adverse action within 12 months of protected activity.
other_provisions.self_help_prohibition: N.C.G.S. §42-25.9: Landlord cannot self-help evict. Must use judicial process. Wrongful exclusion or utility shutoff: tenant entitled to actual damages + reasonable attorney's fees.
other_provisions.smoke_detectors: §42-42(a)(5): Landlord must provide operable smoke detectors at commencement of each tenancy; tenant responsible for maintenance during tenancy.
other_provisions.no_just_cause: North Carolina has no statewide just cause eviction requirement. Landlord may choose not to renew at end of lease term.
other_provisions.application_fees: No statutory cap on application/screening fees in North Carolina.

security_deposit.interest_on_deposit: Must pay interest on deposits held > 5 years (N.C.G.S. § 42-50)
security_deposit.notes: Deposits must be held in trust account; landlord may withhold for unpaid rent/damages beyond ordinary wear

rent_control.notes: No statewide or local rent control. NC state law preempts local rent control.

landlord_entry.notes: No specific statutory period; common law requires reasonable notice

habitability.notes: No repair-and-deduct right in NC. Tenant may terminate for uninhabitable conditions.

security_deposit.max_amount: 1 month's rent (or monthly damage payment, whichever is greater); additional pet deposit allowed
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Other charges authorized by the lease

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

security_deposit.max_amount: 1 month's rent (no pet); 1.25 months' rent (with pet)
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

security_deposit.max_amount: 1 month's rent or $100, whichever is greater
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Other charges authorized by the lease

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.title: New Jersey Residential Landlord-Tenant Law
jurisdictions.governing_statutes: Anti-Eviction Act — N.J.S.A. § 2A:18-61.1 et seq.; Security Deposits — N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-19 to 46:8-26; Habitability — N.J.S.A. §§ 2A:42-10.10 to 10.12; Truth in Renting Act — N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-43 to 46:8-50; Early Termination DV — N.J.S.A. § 46:8-9.7

allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
security_deposit.interest_on_deposit: Required — must be held in interest-bearing account; interest paid annually to tenant
security_deposit.notes: 5 business days return deadline in case of fire, flood, condemnation, or evacuation

eviction.estimated_timeline: 4-8 weeks uncontested; longer for contested cases
eviction.notes: NJ Anti-Eviction Act requires just cause for all evictions: nonpayment, lease violation, tenant at fault, or landlord good faith use (limited)

rent_control.notes: Over 100 NJ municipalities have some form of local rent control; Newark, Jersey City, and many others have ordinances

landlord_entry.advance_notice_required: Reasonable notice implied; no specific statutory period in base statute
landlord_entry.notes: NJ courts have recognized tenant's right to privacy; emergency entry permitted

habitability.notes: Landlord must maintain fit and habitable premises; tenant remedies include rent escrow, repair and deduct, and rent withholding

lease_termination.early_termination_by_tenant: Military SCRA; domestic violence victims under N.J.S.A. 46:8-9.7
lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: Tenant may owe remaining rent; landlord has duty to mitigate damages

statutory_citations: Marini v. Ireland, 56 N.J. 130 (1970) (Implied Warranty of Habitability)

jurisdictions.security_deposit_section: New Jersey caps security deposits at 1.5 months' rent. Landlords must return deposits within 30 days with an itemized statement of deductions. Deposits must be held in a federally insured interest-bearing account, with interest paid annually to the tenant. In cases involving fire, flood, condemnation, or evacuation, the deadline is reduced to 5 business days.
jurisdictions.eviction_section: All evictions must be based on just cause under the Anti-Eviction Act (N.J.S.A. § 2A:18-61.1 et seq.). Court process is required. Just causes include: nonpayment, habitability violations by tenant, tenant at fault, or landlord good faith use (owner move-in, demolition, conversion). Landlord use evictions require relocation assistance in many municipalities.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section: No specific statutory cap on late fees; governed by lease terms and the Truth in Renting Act (N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-43 to 46:8-50). Fees must be reasonable and disclosed.
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section: No statewide rent control. Over 100 municipalities have local rent control ordinances. Rent increase notice requirements vary by municipality. Rent increases on rent-controlled units are typically capped by local ordinance.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section: No specific statutory notice period, but NJ courts recognize a tenant's right to privacy. Landlords may enter only for reasonable purposes (repairs, inspections shown to tenant, emergency) with reasonable advance notice. Emergency entry is permitted without prior notice.
jurisdictions.habitability_section: New Jersey recognizes an implied warranty of habitability (Marini v. Ireland, 56 N.J. 130 (1970)) and codified landlord duties under N.J.S.A. § 2A:42-10.10. Landlords must keep premises fit and habitable. Tenants have remedies including rent escrow, repair and deduct (costs over $100 and under one month's rent, with 10-day notice), and rent withholding. Retaliatory eviction is prohibited.
jurisdictions.lease_termination_section: Month-to-month tenancies require 30 days' notice from either party. The Anti-Eviction Act limits landlord termination rights. Tenants may terminate early under SCRA (military) or N.J.S.A. § 46:8-9.7 (domestic violence). Early termination penalties may include remaining rent, but landlord has a duty to mitigate.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section: NJ Truth in Renting Act (N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-43 to 46:8-50) requires landlords to provide tenants with a copy of the state's Truth in Renting notice before or at lease signing.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: No statewide rent control. Municipalities may adopt local rent control ordinances. Over 100 municipalities have some form of rent control covering certain residential units.
jurisdictions.other_provisions: Domestic Violence: N.J.S.A. § 46:8-9.7 allows domestic violence victims to terminate a lease with 30 days' notice and may recover a pro-rated security deposit refund. Military: SCRA protections apply. Lead Paint: Federal RRP Rule applies; NJ also has state disclosure requirements.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: New Jersey provides strong tenant protections. The Anti-Eviction Act requires just cause for all evictions. Security deposits are capped at 1.5 months with mandatory interest payments. Over 100 municipalities have local rent control ordinances. Tenants have repair and deduct, rent withholding, and rent escrow remedies. Domestic violence victims and military personnel have special early termination rights. The implied warranty of habitability (established by Marini v. Ireland) requires landlords to maintain fit and habitable premises.

security_deposit.max_amount: Under 1 year: 1 month's rent; 1 year or more: no statutory limit
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Utilities and late fees
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Costs due to lease breach
Cleaning costs

lease_termination.early_termination_by_tenant: SCRA for military; 30-day notice for domestic violence victims
lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Nevada Revised Statutes Chapter 118A – Landlord and Tenant: Dwellings
jurisdictions.notes: Nevada enacted significant tenant protections in 2025 (SB 302, effective 2025). No statewide rent control, but local ordinances may apply in Las Vegas/Reno. Surety bond option available as alternative to security deposit. 30-day return rule with up to 30-day penalty.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: NRS Chapter 118A covers all residential tenancy aspects in Nevada. Key sections include 118A.240 (security defined), 118A.242 (security deposit cap, 3 months), 118A.250 (receipt requirements), 118A.260 (landlord disclosure), 118A.290 (habitability), 118A.300 (rent increase notice), 118A.330 (landlord access), 118A.340 (military/incapacity termination).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
NRS 118A.240 'Security' defined. 'Security' means any payment, deposit, fee or charge that is to be used for any of the following purposes: (a) Remedy the tenant's default in the payment of rent; (b) Repair damages to the premises caused by the tenant, excluding ordinary wear and tear; (c) Clean the dwelling unit.
NRS 118A.242 Security: Limitation on amount or value; surety bond in lieu of security; duties and liability of landlord; damages; disputing itemized statement.
(1) A landlord shall not demand or receive as a security deposit an amount or value in excess of 3 months' rent. For purposes of this section, a surety bond or guaranty which is purchased in lieu of a security deposit is considered part of the security deposit.
(2) In lieu of a security deposit, a tenant may, with the consent of the landlord, purchase a surety bond or guaranty to cover the tenant's potential liability for: (a) Default in the payment of rent; (b) Damage to the premises caused by the tenant, excluding ordinary wear and tear; (c) Cleaning the dwelling unit.
(3) A landlord is not obligated to accept a surety bond or guaranty and shall not require a tenant to purchase a surety bond or guaranty in lieu of a security deposit.
(4) Within 30 days after termination of the rental agreement, the landlord shall: (a) Deliver to the tenant the full security deposit or an accounting of the deductions claimed from the security deposit, together with any refunds due; and (b) Provide a written itemized statement of the deductions claimed and the remaining balance.
(5) The tenant may, within 30 days after receipt of the itemized statement, dispute any deductions by providing written notice to the landlord or the landlord's agent. The surety company may not report the claim to a consumer reporting agency before a court determination.
(6) If the landlord fails to deliver the security deposit within 30 days, the landlord is liable for: (a) The full amount of the security deposit; and (b) Up to the full amount of the security deposit in damages as determined by the court.
(7) The court shall consider factors including whether the landlord acted in good faith and the extent of the harm to the tenant.
(8) A provision of a rental agreement that purports to waive any right of the tenant or to impose a penalty on the tenant for withholding a security deposit for a lawful purpose is void.
(9) A tenant's claim to a security deposit has priority over the claims of the landlord's creditors.

eviction_section.statutes: NRS Chapter 118A; eviction governed by NRS Chapter 40 (Forcible and Detainer) and NRS 118A.340 et seq.

notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: Month-to-month: 30-day notice to terminate (NRS 118A.300 requires advance notice before rent increase)
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 14-day notice to cure or quit for noncompliance (NRS 118A.390)

eviction_section.full_text: NRS 118A.350 Landlord's right to terminate rental agreement. The landlord may terminate the rental agreement: (a) For a material breach by the tenant; (b) For nonpayment of rent, fees, or other charges, after 7 days written notice; (c) For a tenant's noncompliance that materially affects health and safety, after 14 days written notice to cure; (d) For repeated violations, after 30 days written notice; (e) For no cause on a month-to-month tenancy, with 30 days written notice.

landlord_entry_section.full_text:
NRS 118A.330 Landlord's access to dwelling unit.
(1) A tenant shall not unreasonably refuse to allow the landlord to enter the dwelling unit to: (a) Inspect the premises; (b) Make ordinary, necessary or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; (c) Supply necessary or agreed services; (d) Exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workmen or contractors; or (e) If a tenant is absent and the landlord reasonably believes that entry is necessary to protect the premises or property.
(2) In an emergency, the landlord may enter without consent.
(3) For non-emergency entry, the landlord shall provide at least 24 hours advance written notice to the tenant and may enter only during normal business hours or reasonable times. The tenant may voluntarily consent to a shorter notice period or entry outside normal business hours.

habitability_section.full_text:
NRS 118A.290 Habitability of dwelling unit.
(1) A landlord shall at all times during the tenancy maintain the dwelling unit in a habitable condition. A dwelling unit is not habitable if it is: (a) Not waterproofed and weather-resistant, including roof and exterior walls with doors and windows; (b) Without plumbing facilities that conformed to applicable law at the time of installation and maintained in good working order; (c) Without adequate water supply, including hot water; (d) Without heating facilities that conformed to applicable law at the time of installation and maintained in good working order; (e) Without electrical lighting, wiring and equipment that conformed to applicable law at the time of installation and maintained in good working order; (f) Without adequate garbage containers and removal services; (g) Without clean and sanitary conditions in the building, grounds and areas under the landlord's control; (h) Without floors, walls, ceilings, stairs and railings maintained in good repair; (i) Without ventilation, air conditioning and elevators, if provided, maintained in good working order.
(2) A landlord and tenant may agree in writing that the tenant is responsible for certain maintenance tasks, minor repairs, and improvements, provided the agreement is made in good faith and does not diminish the landlord's obligations to other tenants.

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Nevada has no statewide rent control. Local governments may not impose rent control (NRS 118B.020 for mobile home parks is preempted). Las Vegas and Reno have no local rent control ordinances as of 2025.

late_fees_section.full_text: NRS 118A.190 Late rent; fees and penalties. A landlord may charge a reasonable late fee for rent that is not paid within 5 days after the date it is due, provided the fee is specified in the written rental agreement. The fee must not be excessive and must reflect actual damages. Nevada courts have interpreted that late fees must be reasonable estimates of uncertain damages.

key_disclosures: NRS 118A.255: Landlord must disclose known material defects in the dwelling unit before lease execution.
NRS 118A.260: Landlord must disclose name and address of owner or agent for service of process.
NRS 118A.275: Must disclose if property is subject to foreclosure proceedings.

other_provisions.military_termination: NRS 118A.340: Active duty military may terminate with 30 days written notice if entering military service.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: NRS 118A.345: Domestic violence victims may terminate lease with 30 days notice and protective order.
other_provisions.retaliation: NRS 118A.510: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: New York Real Property Law (RPP) Article 7 §§ 220–238-A; Real Property Law §§ 226-C, 231-B, 231-C; Emergency Tenant Protection Act (ETPA); Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act of 2019 (HSTPA); NYC Administrative Code Title 26 (NYC Rent Stabilization)
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025-2026 (Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act fully in effect; multiple amendments through 2025)
jurisdictions.notes: New York has one of the most complex landlord-tenant regimes in the US. NYC and many municipalities have rent stabilization/rent control. HSTPA (2019) fundamentally restructured security deposit rules, notice requirements, and eviction protections statewide. The Good Cause Eviction Law (2024) extended just cause protections to nearly all NYC renters and some upstate municipalities.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
N.Y. Real Property Law § 7-108 (as amended by HSTPA 2019).

Sec. 7-108. (1)(a) For dwelling units subject to rent regulation: security deposit may NOT exceed one month's rent.
(b) For all other residential tenancies: HSTPA 2019 capped security deposits at ONE MONTH'S RENT statewide (effective June 14, 2019). This applies to all residential tenants in New York.

(1-a) Where a tenant pays a security deposit: (a) the landlord shall provide the tenant with a receipt; (b) the receipt shall state the banking institution where the deposit is held and the amount of the deposit; (c) within 14 days after the tenant vacates the premises, the landlord must provide the tenant with: (i) an itemized statement of any deductions from the security deposit; and (ii) the balance of the deposit due to the tenant. If the landlord fails to provide the itemized statement within 14 days, landlord forfeits the right to retain any portion of the deposit.

(2)(a) Any deposit: (i) shall be deposited in an interest-bearing account; (ii) the tenant shall receive annual interest at the prevailing passbook savings rate; (iii) the landlord may retain no more than 1% per year as administration fee.
(b) Exception: Landlords owning 6 or fewer units are not required to hold deposits in interest-bearing accounts.

Sec. 7-108(e). The landlord shall not commingle the security deposit with his or her personal funds.

[NYC-specific: NYC Admin Code §26-1201 et seq. governs security deposits for rent stabilized/rent controlled units. Housing Court routinely enforces 14-day return requirement and awards double damages for bad-faith withholding.]
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
New York does not have a specific statewide statute governing landlord entry notice periods for standard residential rentals outside rent-regulated housing. Entry requirements are generally governed by the lease agreement and common law.

For rent-regulated housing: NYC Rent Stabilization Code (RSC) §2523.4 provides that a landlord shall provide reasonable written notice before entering.

Industry standard per NYC practice: 24 hours reasonable advance notice except emergencies.

N.Y. Real Property Law § 235-D. HARASSMENT. (a) It shall be unlawful for any landlord, or any party acting on behalf of a landlord, to engage or threaten to engage in conduct that: (1) would reasonably cause a tenant to vacate a dwelling unit or waive any rights under the rental agreement or applicable law; (2) constitutes harassment.
(b) Harassment includes: removing or wilfully allowing removal of services; interrupting essential services; compelling a tenant to vacate through the use of intimidation, abuse of process, bribery, or willful denial of services.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
N.Y. Real Property Law § 235-B. WARRANTY OF HABITABILITY.
(1) In every written or oral lease or rental agreement for residential premises the landlord or lessor shall be deemed to covenant and warrant that the premises so leased or rented and all areas used in connection therewith in common with other tenants or residents are fit for human habitation and for the uses reasonably intended by the parties and that the occupants of such premises shall not be subjected to any conditions which would be dangerous, hazardous or detrimental to their life, health or safety. When any such condition has been caused by the misconduct of the tenant or lessee or persons under his direction or control, it shall not constitute a breach of such covenants and warranties.
(2) Any agreement by a lessee or tenant of a dwelling waiving or modifying his rights as set forth in this section shall be void as contrary to public policy.

[NYC: NYC Admin Code §27-2000 et seq. (Housing Maintenance Code) sets specific habitability standards including: heat (68°F from Oct 1–May 31, 6am–10pm; 55°F 10pm–6am), hot water (120°F at all times), elevator service, extermination, window guards, etc.]
jurisdictions.rent_control_section:
Statewide rent increases for stabilized units are set annually by local Rent Guidelines Boards (RGB). NYC RGB sets annual increases for ~1 million rent-stabilized units.

2024-2025 NYC RGB: 2.75% for 1-year leases; 5.25% for 2-year leases.

N.Y. Real Property Law § 226-C: All landlords (regardless of rent regulation status) must give 30, 60, or 90 days notice before rent increase or nonrenewal, depending on length of tenancy.

Good Cause Eviction (2024): Rent increase exceeding 5% above CPI is presumed unreasonable for covered units. Covered units: approximately one-third of NYC rental units not already subject to rent stabilization.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
N.Y. Real Property Law § 231-B. FLOOD HISTORY AND RISK NOTICE.
Landlord must provide written notice to tenant BEFORE executing lease: (1) whether the premises are located in a special flood hazard area; (2) whether the premises have been damaged by flooding in the past 5 years; (3) link to FEMA flood maps. Failure to disclose allows tenant to void lease.

N.Y. Real Property Law § 231-A. SPRINKLER SYSTEM DISCLOSURE. Every lease for residential premises shall include a notice if the premises do or do not have a functioning sprinkler system.

N.Y. Real Property Law § 235-BB. CERTIFICATE OF OCCUPANCY DISCLOSURE. Landlord must provide copy of certificate of occupancy or written statement of any pending proceedings upon request.

N.Y. Real Property Law § 235-J. BEDBUG DISCLOSURE. Landlord must provide written disclosure of bedbug infestation history of the unit and building for the preceding year.

Federal lead paint disclosure (42 U.S.C. §4852d): Required for pre-1978 housing.

other_provisions.retaliation: N.Y. Real Property Law §223-B: Landlord may not threaten or take adverse action against tenant for good faith complaints to government agencies, exercising statutory rights, or participating in tenant organizations. Presumption of retaliation for adverse action within 90 days of protected activity.
other_provisions.subletting: N.Y. Real Property Law §226-B: Tenant in a building of 4 or more units may sublet with landlord's consent; landlord may not unreasonably withhold consent.
