other_provisions.domestic_violence: N.Y. Real Property Law §227-C: Victim of domestic violence may terminate lease with 30-day notice and documentation.
other_provisions.attorney_fees: N.Y. Real Property Law §234: If lease provides for landlord attorney fees, tenant has reciprocal right to attorney fees.
other_provisions.HSTPA_2019_key_changes: HSTPA (2019) major reforms: (1) Security deposits capped at 1 month everywhere; (2) Application/credit check fees capped at $20 or actual cost; (3) Guaranteed 14-day itemized statement after move-out; (4) Grace period of 5 days before late fee; (5) Late fees capped at $50 or 5%; (6) 30/60/90 day advance notice for rent increases and nonrenewals; (7) Prohibits unreasonable lease provisions.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Ohio Revised Code Chapter 5321 – Landlords and Tenants
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (last major amendment 2016; SB 271 effective 2024 added eviction notice extensions)
jurisdictions.notes: Ohio is a landlord-friendly state with no statewide rent control or just cause eviction requirement. Local cities (Cleveland, Columbus, Cincinnati) have enacted local tenant protections including rent increase notice requirements and relocation assistance in some cases. Security deposit interest requirements were eliminated in 2016.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Ohio Revised Code Chapter 5321 governs residential tenancies. Key sections: 5321.01 (definitions), 5321.02 (implied warranty of habitability), 5321.03 (landlord obligations), 5321.04 (tenant obligations), 5321.05 (landlord entry), 5321.06 (rental agreement terms), 5321.07 (tenant remedies), 5321.08 (landlord remedies), 5321.09 (eviction), 5321.10 (retaliation prohibited).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.06. Terms and conditions of rental agreement. (A) The landlord and tenant may include in a rental agreement any terms and conditions, including terms and conditions relating to rent and the duration of the agreement, that are not inconsistent with or prohibited by Chapter 5321 or by any other rule of law. (B) Rent increase. If the landlord increases the rent, the tenant may terminate the rental agreement by giving the landlord written notice of termination at least thirty days before the effective date of the increase.
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.16. Landlord's duties. (A) The landlord shall comply with all applicable provisions of any building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety; (B) make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (C) keep all common areas of the premises in a safe and sanitary condition; (D) maintain in good and safe working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances; (E) provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal in the case of four or more units in the building; (F) supply running water and reasonable amounts of hot water at all times and reasonable heat unless the building is not required by law to be equipped for that purpose, or the heat or hot water is generated by an building heating or water-heating system in the premises or by another system under the tenant's exclusive control; (G) not abuse the right of access conferred by section 5321.05 of the Revised Code; (H) give reasonable notice before entry, which shall be presumed to be 24 hours for non-emergency entry; (I) except in the case of a emergency or when the tenant has abandoned or surrendered the premises, may not enter the premises except at a reasonable time. (J) The landlord may not engage in retaliatory conduct under section 5321.02 or 5321.03.
Note: Ohio eliminated the security deposit interest requirement in 2016 (HB 390). Landlords are no longer required to pay interest on security deposits.

notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy
notice_requirements.drug_criminal_activity: Immediate termination possible with 30-day notice if tenant allows drug activity (R.C. 5321.17)

eviction_section.full_text:
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.08. Landlord's remedies for tenant's breach. (A) If the tenant fails to comply with the obligations of sections 5321.04 to 5321.05 of the Revised Code or the tenant's obligations under the rental agreement, the landlord may: (1) Recover damages caused by the tenant's breach; (2) Bring an action for rent that is due; (3) Terminate the rental agreement and bring an action for possession under Chapter 1923 of the Revised Code; (4) Take any other action that is appropriate to enforce the landlord's rights.
(B) If the landlord brings an action for possession under Chapter 1923 and the court finds that the tenant has failed to pay rent when due, the court may issue a writ of restitution placing the landlord in possession of the premises.
R.C. 1923.04. Notice to vacate. In an action for nonpayment of rent, the landlord shall give the tenant three days' notice to leave the premises. If the premises are used as a dwelling unit by the tenant, the notice shall be served upon the tenant or left at the tenant's usual place of residence at least three days before the hearing.

landlord_entry_section.full_text:
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.05. Tenant's obligations. (A) The tenant shall: ...(7) Not unreasonably refuse to allow the landlord to enter the dwelling unit pursuant to division (B) of this section.
(B) The landlord may enter the dwelling unit if: (1) The entry is at a reasonable time; (2) The landlord gives the tenant reasonable notice before entering, which shall be presumed to be twenty-four hours; and (3) The entry is for one of the following purposes: (a) To inspect the premises; (b) To make ordinary, necessary, or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; (c) To supply necessary or agreed services; (d) To exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workmen, or contractors; (e) If the tenant is absent from the premises and the landlord reasonably believes that entry is necessary to protect the premises or property. (C) The tenant shall not abuse the right of access.
Landlord's remedies for violation: If the tenant violates this section, the landlord may recover actual damages and reasonable attorney fees.

habitability_section.full_text:
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.04. Tenant's obligations. The tenant shall: (1) Comply with all applicable building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety; (2) Keep the premises safe and sanitary; (3) Dispose of garbage properly; (4) Not deliberately or negligently destroy, deface, damage, or remove any part of the premises; (5) Not permit a person to remain in the premises who willfully or carelessly destroys, defaces, or damages the premises; (6) Not engage in or permit drug activity prohibited under Chapter 2925 of the Revised Code.
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.03. Landlord's obligations. The landlord shall: (1) Comply with all applicable building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety; (2) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep all common areas of the premises in a safe and sanitary condition; (4) Maintain in good and safe working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances; (5) Provide garbage containers and arrange for removal (4+ unit buildings); (6) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times and reasonable heat unless not required by law; (7) Not abuse access rights; (8) Give reasonable notice before entry (presumed 24 hours); (9) Not engage in retaliatory conduct.
Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.07. Failure of landlord to fulfill obligations. If a landlord fails to fulfill the obligations of § 5321.04, the tenant may: (1) Give written notice to the landlord specifying the failure; (2) After reasonable time (presumed 30 days), deposit rent with the court clerk, apply for an order to compel compliance, or terminate the rental agreement.

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Ohio has no statewide rent control. Local governments may not impose rent control. Columbus, Cleveland, and Cincinnati have rent increase notice requirements but not rent ceilings.

late_fees_section.full_text: Ohio Rev. Code § 5321.06. Terms and conditions of rental agreement. The landlord and tenant may include any terms and conditions in the rental agreement that are not inconsistent with Chapter 5321 or other law. Late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written rental agreement. Ohio courts have held that late fees must bear a reasonable relationship to the actual damages caused by late payment.

key_disclosures: R.C. 5321.02: Implied warranty of habitability cannot be waived in rental agreements. Landlord must disclose and remedy any known violations of building/housing codes at lease execution.
R.C. 5321.19: Landlord must disclose name and address of owner or agent for service of legal process.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law (40 CFR Part 745) for pre-1978 housing.

other_provisions.retaliation: R.C. 5321.10: Landlord may not take retaliatory action against tenant for exercising rights under Chapter 5321, complaining to a governmental entity, or establishing a tenant organization. If landlord takes adverse action within 6 months of tenant's protected activity, there is a rebuttable presumption of retaliation.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: Ohio does not have a statewide statute specifically addressing domestic violence lease termination, but courts have recognized certain defenses.
other_provisions.drug_activity: R.C. 5321.17: If landlord knows or has reasonable cause to believe drug activity is occurring on the premises, landlord may terminate with 30 days notice and must bring eviction action within 30 days.

security_deposit.max_amount: No statutory limit (commonly 1 month's rent in practice)
security_deposit.notes: Ohio has no statewide security deposit cap; landlords commonly request 1 month. Eviction requires 3-day notice for nonpayment under ORC 1923.

rent_control.notes: No statewide rent control. Some municipalities (e.g., Columbus, Cleveland, Athens) have limited local ordinances.

landlord_entry.notes: No specific statutory period; implied by case law

habitability.notes: Tenant must give written notice; landlord has reasonable time to repair.

jurisdictions.title: Oklahoma Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 115

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline: 45 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized statement of deductions within 45 days.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord is required to place the security deposit in a banking institution in Oklahoma, either in a separate account or by posting a surety bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return the deposit within 45 days, tenant may recover actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees. Court may also award punitive damages in bad-faith cases.
- Abandonment: If tenant abandons the premises, landlord may give 5 days notice to claim property and may then dispose of abandoned property.
- Transfer of ownership: New owner is responsible for the security deposit; 30 days notice of transfer required.
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 115: 'The landlord shall return the security deposit within forty-five (45) days after the termination of the tenancy.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, §§ 131–132

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: 5 days written notice to quit. If tenant pays all overdue rent within 5 days, tenancy is reinstated.
- Other lease violations: 10 days written notice specifying the violation.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT lock out, shut off utilities, or remove tenant's belongings without a court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in district or municipal court; summons issued; hearing within days.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, material noncompliance, holding over, nuisance.
- Expedited: For nonpayment, case can move relatively quickly once notice period expires.
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 131: Governs landlord's right to terminate and recover possession for nonpayment of rent.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, §§ 118–121

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Oklahoma law imposes obligations on landlords to maintain habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with housing codes; keep premises in good repair; maintain structural, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; provide garbage receptacles.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: ALLOWED. Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 121: After giving landlord 14 days written notice of needed repairs and landlord fails to repair within that time, tenant may have repairs made and deduct costs from rent.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health and safety and landlord fails to repair within 14 days of notice.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action for damages and injunctive relief.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 127 prohibits retaliation against tenants who exercise their rights.
- Rent control: None. Oklahoma has no statewide rent control.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 122

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Oklahoma law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum hours mandated by statute.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver packages, or inspect the premises.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times, generally during normal business hours.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
Okla. Stat. tit. 41, § 122: Governs landlord's right of entry. Landlord may enter at reasonable times in a reasonable manner for specified purposes.
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Okla. Stat. tit. 41

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in Oklahoma.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Oregon Revised Statutes Chapter 90 – Residential Landlord and Tenant
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (Current through early 2026; includes 2024 regular session changes)
jurisdictions.notes: Oregon has strong tenant protections including statewide rent control (ORS 90.323, 90.324) with 7%+7% annual cap and just cause eviction requirement (ORS 90.427). SB 608 (2019) established statewide just cause eviction. SR 890 (2024) extended eviction notice periods. Portland and other cities have additional local tenant protections.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: ORS Chapter 90 covers all residential tenancy aspects. Key sections include: 90.100-90.160 (definitions, notice), 90.220-90.297 (rental agreements, fees, security deposit), 90.300-90.380 (habitability, maintenance, landlord remedies), 90.392-90.475 (termination, eviction, domestic violence protections), 90.505-90.850 (manufactured dwelling park special rules).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
ORS 90.300 Security deposit. (1) A landlord may not require a tenant to pay a security deposit in an amount or value that exceeds an amount equal to one month's rent, excluding any portion of the rent that is for utilities, if the landlord is furnishing or providing utilities at the beginning of the tenancy. (2) The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written checklist or statement recording the condition of the dwelling unit, including all furnishings and appliances, at the beginning of the tenancy. The tenant may supplement or rebut this checklist. (3) Upon termination of tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant within 31 days after the tenant has vacated the premises, if the landlord retains any portion of the deposit, the landlord shall give the tenant an itemized statement of the basis for and amount of any security deposit retained and return any remaining balance. (4) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 31 days, the tenant may recover the amount of the deposit, regardless of whether there is any actual damage.
ORS 90.302 Permissible fees. A landlord may not charge a tenant any fee other than: (a) A security deposit not to exceed one month's rent; (b) Last month's rent if provided for in the rental agreement; (c) A charge for late payment of rent if the charge does not exceed 5 percent of the monthly rent; (d) A charge for anreturned check if the landlord has previously charged a fee for a returned check or if the charge is made pursuant to a written agreement; (e) A fee authorized by ORS 90.295 (screening fee).

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 4-day notice to pay or vacate for nonpayment (ORS 90.394, extended to 10 days by SR 890 effective 2025)
notice_requirements.no_cause_termination: 30-day notice (90.427) for month-to-month; 90-day notice if tenant has lived there 1+ year
notice_requirements.cause_termination: 14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance (ORS 90.392)
notice_requirements.immediate_termination: 24 hours notice for drug/alcohol violations, violent/criminal activity (ORS 90.396, 90.398)

eviction_section.full_text:
ORS 90.427 No cause termination; notice; reasons prohibited. (1)(a) A landlord shall not terminate a tenancy without cause. The landlord must provide a written notice specifying the reason for termination. (b) If no specific cause exists, the landlord must provide a termination notice of at least 30 days for month-to-month tenancy. If the tenant has resided in the dwelling unit for one year or more, the landlord must provide at least 90 days' notice.
(2) A landlord may not terminate or refuse to renew a tenancy because: (a) The tenant has exercised any right or remedy under this chapter or other law; (b) The tenant has made a complaint to a governmental entity; or (c) The landlord's desire to convert the dwelling unit to another use, unless the landlord pays relocation assistance equal to one month's rent.
ORS 90.392 Termination for cause. If a tenant materially breaches the rental agreement or fails to comply with the obligations of tenancy, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by giving not less than 14 days' written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate if the breach is not cured within 14 days. If the breach is not cured within the 14 days, the rental agreement terminates.
ORS 90.394 Termination for nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give the tenant a notice that the tenant has 4 days (extended to 10 days under SR 890 effective 2025) to pay the rent or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within the notice period, the landlord may proceed with termination.

landlord_entry_section.full_text:
ORS 90.322 Landlord or agent access to dwelling unit. (1) A landlord may enter a dwelling unit only: (a) At a reasonable time; (b) In a reasonable manner; and (c) After proper notice to the tenant, except in emergencies.
(2) Proper notice means: (a) Verbal notice given at the time of entry, if the entry is at a reasonable time; or (b) Written notice left on the dwelling unit at least 24 hours before entry, if the entry is not at a reasonable time.
(3) The landlord may enter without notice in an emergency. (4) The tenant may request that the landlord not enter at certain times, if the request is reasonable and the tenant gives the landlord written notice.
(5) If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, the tenant may recover actual damages or $250, whichever is greater, and reasonable attorney fees.

habitability_section.full_text:
ORS 90.320 Landlord's duty to maintain dwelling unit in habitable condition. (1) A landlord shall maintain a dwelling unit in a habitable condition and shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing codes; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a safe and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas of the premises clean and safe; (d) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems and appliances provided by the landlord; (e) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal if the landlord is responsible for removal; (f) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times.
(2) The landlord is not responsible for conditions caused by the tenant or the tenant's family or guests. (3) If the landlord fails to comply with the obligations of this section, the tenant may: (a) Repair the condition and deduct the cost from rent; (b) Terminate the rental agreement; or (c) Recover actual damages, including damages for diminished rental value.

rent_control_section.summary: Oregon has statewide rent increase limits. Annual rent increases are capped at 7% plus the Consumer Price Index (CPI), limited to a maximum of 10% + CPI. Landlords must give 90 days' notice for rent increases over 7%. Exceptions apply for new construction (first 15 years), single-family homes sold within past 5 years, and subsidized housing.
rent_control_section.full_text: ORS 90.323 Maximum rent increase. (1) A landlord may not increase the rent payable by a tenant by more than 7 percent plus the Consumer Price Index in any 12-month period, not to exceed 10 percent plus the CPI. (2) If a landlord proposes a rent increase exceeding 7 percent, the landlord shall give the tenant at least 90 days' written notice of the proposed increase. (3) This section does not apply to: (a) Dwelling units first certificate of occupancy issued within the previous 15 years; (b) Single-family dwellings that have been sold to a purchaser who intends to occupy the dwelling unit as the purchaser's primary residence; (c) Housing that is subject to federal or state subsidy programs.

late_fees_section.full_text: ORS 90.260 Charges for late payment of rent. (1) A landlord may charge a tenant for late payment of rent only if: (a) The tenant has not paid rent within 5 days after the date the rent was due; and (b) The charge is specified in a written rental agreement. (2) The charge for late payment of rent may not exceed 5 percent of the monthly rent. (3) A provision in a rental agreement that violates this section is unenforceable.

key_disclosures: ORS 90.228: Must disclose if dwelling is in a 100-year flood plain before lease execution.
ORS 90.305: Must disclose information about legal proceedings affecting the property.
ORS 90.310: Must disclose pending legal actions involving the premises.
ORS 90.316: Must install and maintain carbon monoxide alarms.
ORS 90.303: Must disclose basis for rejecting rental application.

other_provisions.domestic_violence: ORS 90.449-90.459: Domestic violence victims have special protections including right to terminate lease early, right to change locks, and protection from eviction based solely on perpetrator's actions.
other_provisions.military_termination: ORS 90.472-90.475: Active duty military may terminate with 30 days notice. Reservists and National Guard members called to active duty may terminate.
other_provisions.retaliation: ORS 90.385: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights.
other_provisions.manufactured_dwelling_parks: ORS 90.505-90.850: Special rules for manufactured dwelling parks including park closure procedures, utility billing, and tenant purchase rights.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Pennsylvania Statutes Title 68 (68 P.S. §§ 250.101–250.602) – Landlord and Tenant Act of 1951, as amended
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (Act 54 of 2024 made changes to eviction procedures; Philadelphia has local just cause ordinance effective 2025)
jurisdictions.notes: Pennsylvania is a landlord-friendly state with no statewide rent control or just cause eviction requirement. Philadelphia enacted a Just Cause Eviction ordinance (Bill No. 240276, effective 2025) requiring just cause for eviction in covered units. Pittsburgh and several other municipalities have local tenant protections.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Pennsylvania's Landlord and Tenant Act of 1951 (68 P.S. §§ 250.101–250.602) governs residential tenancies statewide. Key sections: § 250.101 (definitions), § 250.102 (implied warranty of habitability), § 250.103 (landlord's duties), § 250.104 (tenant's duties and landlord access), § 250.201 (rental agreement), § 250.501–250.512 (security deposit), § 250.601–250.602 (eviction remedies).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
68 P.S. § 250.101. Definitions. 'Security deposit' means any payment of money given to a landlord by or on behalf of a tenant to secure the performance of any obligation of the tenant under a rental agreement. 'Tenant' means a person who is a lessee in possession of premises. 'Landlord' means the owner or manager of a rental premises.
68 P.S. § 250.102. Implied warranty of habitability. In any written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived.
68 P.S. § 250.201. Rental agreement. (a) A rental agreement may contain any terms and conditions not prohibited by this act or other law, including terms providing for the establishment of a security deposit. (b) A security deposit may not exceed two months' rent for the first year and one month's rent for renewals, except in Philadelphia where different limits may apply.
68 P.S. § 250.511a. Escrow funds limited. (a) A landlord who receives a security deposit shall deposit the money in a fiduciary capacity in a banking institution in Pennsylvania, to be held in trust for the deposit of the tenant. (b) Within thirty (30) days of the termination of the tenant's occupancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions. (c) The landlord shall give the tenant an itemized list of deductions and the balance due.
68 P.S. § 250.512. Recovery of improperly held escrow funds. (a) If a landlord fails to return the security deposit within thirty (30) days, the tenant may recover the full amount of the security deposit as a debt in an action brought in the court of common pleas. (b) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit in bad faith, the tenant may recover treble damages.

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 10-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (Act 54 of 2024 extended from 7 to 10 days)
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 30-day notice for noncompliance under the act
notice_requirements.no_cause: Philadelphia covered units require just cause (Bill No. 240276, effective 2025)

eviction_section.full_text:
68 P.S. § 250.601. Landlord's remedies for breach. If the tenant fails to comply with the tenant's obligations under this act or the rental agreement, the landlord may: (1) Terminate the tenancy and bring an action for possession; (2) Recover damages caused by the tenant's breach; (3) Bring an action for rent that is due.
68 P.S. § 250.602. Recovery of possession limited. No landlord may recover possession of a dwelling unit unless the landlord: (1) Has a court order for possession; (2) Complies with all applicable laws regarding the eviction process; (3) Gives proper notice to the tenant as required by law.
Pennsylvania eviction procedures are governed by Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19. A landlord must serve a notice to quit (10 days for nonpayment under Act 54 of 2024) before filing a complaint in ejectment. The court will schedule a hearing and issue a judgment. If the judgment is for the landlord, a writ of possession may be issued.

landlord_entry_section.full_text: 68 P.S. § 250.104. Landlord's right of entry. (a) A landlord may enter the tenant's premises without the tenant's consent in case of emergency. (b) For non-emergency entry, a landlord may enter the tenant's premises at reasonable times upon reasonable notice if: (1) The landlord gives the tenant advance notice; (2) The entry is for a legitimate purpose such as inspection, repair, or showing to prospective tenants or buyers; (3) The entry is at a reasonable time. Pennsylvania courts have generally recognized that reasonable notice means at least 24 hours. (c) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent for the landlord to enter.

habitability_section.full_text:
68 P.S. § 250.102. Implied warranty of habitability. In any written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived.
68 P.S. § 250.103. Landlord's duties. The landlord shall: (1) Comply with all applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety; (2) Make all repairs necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep all common areas in a safe and sanitary condition; (4) Maintain all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances in good working order; (5) Provide running water and reasonable hot water and heat unless the building is not required by law to have these facilities or the utilities are under the tenant's exclusive control.
If the landlord fails to comply with the implied warranty of habitability, the tenant may: (a) Bring an action for damages; (b) Withhold rent and deposit it in court; (c) Terminate the lease; (d) Make repairs and deduct the cost from rent (limited to the lesser of the cost of repairs or one month's rent).

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Pennsylvania has no statewide rent control. Philadelphia has a rent increase complaint procedure but no hard rent ceilings. Philadelphia's Just Cause Eviction ordinance (2025) requires just cause for eviction but does not cap rents.

late_fees_section.full_text: 68 P.S. § 250.201. Rental agreement. A rental agreement may contain terms and conditions not prohibited by this act or other law. Late fees must be specified in the written rental agreement. Pennsylvania courts have held that late fees must be reasonable and may be reduced or voided as penalties if excessive. There is no statutory cap on late fees, but courts apply a reasonableness standard.

key_disclosures: § 250.206: Landlord must provide a statement of escrowed funds if the landlord is escrowing security deposits with a financial institution.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law for pre-1978 housing.
Philadelphia local ordinance: Landlord must register with the City and provide registration information to tenants.
Philadelphia: Must disclose any pending housing code violations.

other_provisions.retaliation: 68 P.S. § 250.120-A: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights under this act. If landlord takes adverse action within 6 months of tenant's protected activity, presumption of retaliation applies.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: Pennsylvania Protection from Abuse Act (23 Pa.C.S. § 6101 et seq.) provides for exclusion of abuser from residence and lease modifications.
other_provisions.mitigation: Landlord has duty to mitigate damages by attempting to re-rent after tenant breach.

allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear

lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.title: South Carolina Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
S.C. Code § 27-40-410

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount as security deposit.
- Return deadline: 30 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Yes. Landlord must provide a written, itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, other amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Landlord must deposit security deposit in a banking institution in South Carolina in an account separate from operating funds, or post a surety bond.
- Interest: No interest required to be paid to tenant on security deposits.
- Landlord disclosure: Landlord must disclose the location of the security deposit account and the tenant's rights.
- Penalty: If landlord fails to return deposit within 30 days, tenant may recover actual damages and attorney's fees. Court may award punitive damages if landlord acted in bad faith.
- Transfer of ownership: New landlord is liable for return of deposit; must notify tenant within 30 days.
S.C. Code § 27-40-410: 'The landlord shall return the security deposit within thirty days after the termination of the tenancy and delivery of possession of the premises, less any lawful deductions.'
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
S.C. Code §§ 27-40-510 to 27-40-530

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through the court system.
- Nonpayment of rent: 5 days written notice to quit. If tenant pays overdue rent within 5 days, tenancy is reinstated.
- Lease violation: 14 days written notice specifying violation, with 14 days right to cure.
- Month-to-month termination: 30 days written notice required.
- Holdover: After lease expiration, tenant becomes a month-to-month tenant.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT use self-help measures; must obtain court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested cases approximately 2-4 weeks.
- Court process: Landlord files in magistrate court (for possession and rent up to $7,500) or circuit court; tenant served with summons.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment of rent, material noncompliance, end of lease term, nuisance or illegal activity.
S.C. Code § 27-40-510: Landlord may bring an action for rent and possession after proper notice has been given.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
S.C. Code §§ 27-40-310 to 27-40-340

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: S.C. Code § 27-40-310 imposes a duty on landlords to maintain fit and habitable premises.
- Landlord's duty: Comply with applicable building and housing codes; keep premises in good repair; maintain structural components, plumbing, heating, electrical systems; provide running water and hot water; provide garbage collection.
- Tenant's remedies:
  1. Repair and deduct: NOT ALLOWED under South Carolina law.
  2. Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant cannot unilaterally withhold rent for habitability issues.
  3. Termination: Tenant may terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair health or safety.
  4. Court action: Tenant may bring action to enforce landlord's obligations or seek damages.
  5. Code enforcement: Tenant may report violations to local building inspections department.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. S.C. Code § 27-40-910 prohibits landlords from evicting in retaliation for tenant's complaints or exercise of rights.
- Rent control: None. South Carolina has no statewide or local rent control.
- Building codes: Landlords must comply with applicable local housing and building codes.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
S.C. Code § 27-40-310

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written lease.
- Amount: No statutory maximum; must be reasonable and specified in the lease.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the rental agreement.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
S.C. Code § 27-40-340

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: South Carolina law requires 'reasonable notice' before entry. No specific minimum hours mandated.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, deliver items, or inspect the premises.
- Entry to show: Landlord may enter to show premises to prospective tenants, buyers, or lenders.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Entry times: Entry should be at reasonable times, generally during normal business hours.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent to entry.
S.C. Code § 27-40-340: 'The landlord may enter the dwelling unit only at reasonable times in a reasonable manner for the purpose of making repairs, providing services, delivering packages, or exhibiting the dwelling unit.'
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
S.C. Code §§ 27-40-210 to 27-40-230

Key provisions:
- Rent control: NO statewide rent control in South Carolina.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 30 days advance written notice.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases.
- Landlord may raise rent by any amount with proper notice.

Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Turnover cleaning costs

tenant_remedies.rent_reduction: Seek through local code enforcement or court

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Tennessee Code Annotated Title 66, Chapter 28 – Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.notes: Tennessee is a landlord-friendly state with no statewide rent control or just cause eviction requirement. Memphis has local tenant protections including source-of-income discrimination prohibition and just cause eviction requirements for some units. HB 747 (2023) made minor changes to security deposit provisions. SB 731 (2023) addressed squatters' rights.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Tennessee's URLTA (T.C.A. §§ 66-28-101 to 66-28-522) governs residential tenancies. Key sections include: 66-28-201 (landlord obligations), 66-28-301 (security deposit), 66-28-302 (landlord/agent address, effective 1/1/2025), 66-28-401 (tenant obligations), 66-28-403 (landlord access), 66-28-501 to 66-28-521 (various remedies and eviction procedures).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
T.C.A. § 66-28-301. Security deposit.
(a) Upon request by the landlord for a tenant to vacate or within five (5) days after receipt by the landlord of written notice of the tenant's intent to vacate, the landlord shall notify the tenant of the tenant's right to be present at the landlord's inspection of the premises to determine the tenant's liability for physical damages that are the basis for any charge against the security deposit and the landlord's estimated costs to cure such damages.
(b) The landlord shall conduct the inspection within a reasonable time after the tenant vacates. If the tenant desires to be present at the inspection, the tenant shall notify the landlord, and the landlord shall conduct a joint inspection with the tenant and compile a comprehensive listing of any presently observable damage to the premises. Both the landlord and the tenant shall sign the listing and shall receive a copy. Such signed listing shall be conclusive evidence of the accuracy of the listing in any subsequent dispute regarding the security deposit.
(c) If the landlord conducts the inspection without the tenant present: (i) If the tenant has not notified the landlord of the tenant's intent to be present, the landlord may proceed without the tenant; (ii) If the tenant has abandoned the premises; (iii) If a court orders the tenant to vacate; (iv) If the tenant notifies the landlord that the tenant does not wish to be present; (v) If the tenant fails to appear for a scheduled inspection; or (vi) If the tenant does not request to be present.
(d) If the landlord fails to comply with this section by not providing a checklist of damages, the landlord is prohibited from recovering any portion of the security deposit as damages.
(e) Disposition. If the tenant owes no rent or other charges and there is a refund due the tenant, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant, by first class mail to the tenant's last known address, a refund and a written statement itemizing the basis for, and describing the condition and circumstances of, any damages and the estimated cost to cure the damages. If the tenant does not respond within sixty (60) days after the delivery of the statement, the landlord may retain the deposit.
(f) Additional damages. If additional damages are discovered within thirty (30) days after the tenant vacates or within seven (7) days after a new tenant moves in, whichever is earlier, the landlord may recover the cost of repairs from the tenant.
(g) Disclosure. At the time of execution of the rental agreement and delivery of the deposit, the landlord shall disclose to the tenant the location of the account in which the deposit is held, but is not required to disclose the account number.

notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 30-day notice to cure or quit for noncompliance (T.C.A. § 66-28-507)
notice_requirements.unlawful_detainer: Notice and court action required under T.C.A. § 29-18-101 et seq.

eviction_section.full_text:
T.C.A. § 66-28-505. Failure to pay rent. (a) If rent is due and unpaid, the landlord may give written notice to the tenant requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises within fourteen (14) days after the delivery of the notice to the tenant. (b) The notice shall specify that the tenant has fourteen (14) days to pay rent or vacate. If the tenant fails to pay rent or vacate within the fourteen-day period, the landlord may bring an action for possession and rent.
T.C.A. § 66-28-507. Noncompliance. (a) If the tenant materially fails to comply with the rental agreement or fails to fulfill the tenant's obligations under § 66-28-401, the landlord may give written notice specifying the tenant's acts or omissions and stating that the rental agreement will terminate upon a date not less than thirty (30) days after receipt of the notice. (b) If the failure is remediable and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of the notice, the rental agreement does not terminate. (c) If the breach is not remediable by repair or the payment of damages, the landlord may give written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate upon a date not less than fourteen (14) days after receipt of the notice.

landlord_entry_section.full_text: T.C.A. § 66-28-403. Access by landlord. (a) The tenant shall not unreasonably withhold consent to the landlord to enter the dwelling unit. (b) The landlord may enter the dwelling unit without consent of the tenant if: (1) The entry is at a reasonable time; (2) The landlord gives the tenant reasonable notice before entering; and (3) The entry is for one of the following purposes: (i) To inspect the premises; (ii) To make ordinary, necessary, or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements; (iii) To supply necessary or agreed services; (iv) To exhibit the dwelling unit to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workmen, or contractors; (v) If the tenant is absent from the premises and the landlord reasonably believes that entry is necessary to protect the premises or property. (c) The landlord may enter without notice in the case of an emergency. (d) The landlord shall not abuse the right of access conferred by this section. (e) Reasonable notice for non-emergency entry is presumed to be at least twenty-four (24) hours.

habitability_section.full_text:
T.C.A. § 66-28-201. Landlord's obligations. (a) The landlord shall: (1) Comply with all applicable building and housing code requirements that materially affect health and safety; (2) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep all common areas of the premises in a clean and safe condition; (4) Maintain in good and safe working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air conditioning, and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, provided by the landlord; (5) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal in the case of two (2) or more dwelling units; (6) Supply running water and reasonable amounts of hot water at all times and reasonable heat unless the heat hot water is generated by a building heating or water-heating system under the tenant's exclusive control; (7) Not engage in or permit any drug-related criminal activity as defined in § 40-39-111 on the premises.
T.C.A. § 66-28-202. Implied warranty of habitability. In addition to the obligations imposed by § 66-28-201, there is an implied warranty of habitability that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty cannot be waived.

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Tennessee has no statewide rent control. Local governments are generally prohibited from imposing rent control. Memphis has local source-of-income discrimination protections but no rent ceilings.

late_fees_section.full_text: T.C.A. § 66-28-201 (implied). Late fees must be reasonable and specified in the written rental agreement. Tennessee courts have held that late fees must bear a reasonable relationship to the landlord's actual damages from late payment and must not be punitive. There is no statutory cap on late fees, but courts may void excessive fees as penalties.

key_disclosures: T.C.A. § 66-28-302 (effective January 1, 2025): Landlord or authorized agent's name and address must be disclosed in the rental agreement for service of legal process.
Lead paint disclosure required under federal law for pre-1978 housing.
T.C.A. § 66-28-201: Landlord must disclose any known material code violations before lease execution.

other_provisions.retaliation: T.C.A. § 66-28-521: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant for exercising rights. If landlord takes adverse action within 6 months of tenant's protected activity, presumption of retaliation applies.
other_provisions.drug_activity: T.C.A. § 66-28-201(a)(7): Landlord must not engage in or permit drug-related criminal activity on the premises.
other_provisions.abandonment: T.C.A. § 66-28-401: Tenant must notify landlord if absent for more than 7 days.

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Texas Property Code Title 8, Chapter 92 – Residential Tenancies
jurisdictions.notes: No statewide rent control. No mandatory security deposit cap. Texas is a landlord-friendly state. HB 2107 (2021) added flood plain disclosure (§92.0135). Late fee capped at 12% (≤4 units) or 10% (>4 units) of monthly rent (§92.019).
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Tex. Prop. Code §§ 92.101–92.111

Sec. 92.101. APPLICATION OF SUBCHAPTER. This subchapter applies to a security deposit—however designated—in connection with a residential lease.

Sec. 92.102. SECURITY DEPOSIT. A landlord may not collect from a tenant a sum of money that is denominated as a security deposit in an amount that exceeds the sum of one month's rent. [Note: This cap applies only to deposits explicitly called 'security deposits'; no general cap exists.]

Sec. 92.103. OBLIGATION TO REFUND. (a) Except as provided by Section 92.107, the landlord shall refund a security deposit to the tenant on or before the 30th day after the date the tenant surrenders the premises.

Sec. 92.104. RETENTION OF SECURITY DEPOSIT; ACCOUNTING. (a) Before returning a security deposit, the landlord may deduct from the deposit damages and charges for which the tenant is legally liable under the lease or as a result of breaching the lease. (b) The landlord may not retain any portion of a security deposit to cover normal wear and tear. (c) If the landlord retains all or part of a security deposit, the landlord shall give to the tenant the balance of the deposit, if any, together with a written description and itemized list of all deductions. The landlord is not required to give the description and itemized list if the tenant owes rent when he surrenders the premises and there is no controversy concerning the amount of rent owed.

Sec. 92.109. LIABILITY OF LANDLORD. (a) A landlord who in bad faith retains a security deposit in violation of this subchapter is liable for an amount equal to the sum of $100, three times the portion of the deposit wrongfully withheld, and the tenant's reasonable attorney's fees in a suit to recover the deposit. (b) A landlord who in bad faith does not provide a written description and itemized list of deductions is liable for the same amounts described in Subsection (a). (c) For purposes of this section, a landlord is presumed to have acted in bad faith if the landlord does not provide the description, itemized list of deductions, and any balance of the deposit to the tenant on or before the 30th day after the date the tenant surrenders the premises.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Tex. Prop. Code § 24.005. NOTICE TO VACATE. (a) If the occupant is a tenant under a written lease or oral rental agreement, the landlord must give a tenant who defaults or holds over beyond a rental agreement three days' written notice to vacate the premises before the landlord files a forcible detainer suit, unless the parties have contracted for a shorter or longer notice period in a written lease or agreement. The landlord may not waive or eliminate the right to file a forcible detainer suit by accepting past due rent.

Texas has NO Just Cause requirement for eviction. No-fault eviction is permitted at end of lease term. Month-to-month: notice equal to rental period (min. 1 month per §91.001).

Retaliation Defense – Tex. Prop. Code §92.331: A landlord may not file an eviction proceeding because a tenant in good faith exercises a right or remedy granted by lease or statute, complains to a governmental entity, or participates in a tenant organization.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Texas has no specific statewide advance notice statute for landlord entry. Reasonable notice implied by common law. Self-help eviction prohibited per:

Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.0081. REMOVAL OF PROPERTY AND EXCLUSION OF RESIDENTIAL TENANT. (a) A landlord may not remove a door, window, or attic hatchway cover or a lock, latch, hinge, hinge pin, doorknob, or other mechanism connected to a door, window, or attic hatchway cover from a dwelling unit or remove furniture, fixtures, or appliances furnished by the landlord from a dwelling unit unless the landlord is making a good faith effort to repair or replace [them]. (b) Except as provided by Subsection (g), a landlord may not intentionally prevent a tenant from entering the leased premises except by judicial process.

Unlawful lockout remedy – §92.009: Tenant may recover possession + actual damages + $1,000 + 1 month's rent + attorney fees if locked out without court order.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.052. LANDLORD'S DUTY TO REPAIR OR REMEDY. (a) A landlord shall make a diligent effort to repair or remedy a condition if: (1) the tenant specifies the condition in a notice to the person to whom or to the place where rent is normally paid; (2) the tenant is not delinquent in the payment of rent at the time notice is given; and (3) the condition: (A) materially affects the physical health or safety of an ordinary tenant; or (B) arises from the landlord's failure to provide and maintain in good working order a device to supply hot water of a minimum temperature of 120 degrees Fahrenheit.

Sec. 92.056. LANDLORD LIABILITY AND TENANT REMEDIES. A landlord is liable to a tenant if: (1) the tenant has given notice to repair; (2) the condition materially affects health or safety; (3) a subsequent notice was given after reasonable time; (4) the landlord had a reasonable time to repair (presumed not more than 7 days); (5) the landlord has not made a diligent effort; and (6) the tenant was not delinquent in rent.

Tenant remedies for landlord failure to repair (§92.0561): terminate lease; reduce rent; repair and deduct (up to $500 or one month's rent, whichever is greater); obtain judicial remedies.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Texas has NO statewide rent control. Tex. Prop. Code §214.902 preempts local governments from enacting rent control except during declared disasters.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section: Tex. Prop. Code Sec. 92.019. LATE PAYMENT OF RENT; FEES. (a) A landlord may not charge a tenant a late fee unless: (1) notice of the fee is included in a written lease; (2) the fee is a reasonable estimate of uncertain damages; and (3) the rent remains unpaid after the SECOND day after the date the rent was originally due. (d) Late fee cap: 12% of monthly rent for structures with ≤4 dwelling units; 10% for structures with >4 dwelling units. (e) Landlord who violates is liable to tenant for $100 + three times the illegal late fee + attorney's fees.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
§92.0135 (2021): Flood plain disclosure – if dwelling is located in a 100-year flood plain as designated by FEMA, landlord must disclose in writing before lease execution.
§92.201: Landlord must disclose name and address of property owner or authorized agent in writing at lease commencement.
§92.013: Written notice required before any change of rule or policy that materially affects the tenant's personal property.
§92.024: Landlord must provide copy of lease within 3 business days of tenant's written request.

other_provisions.required_smoke_alarms: §92.254–92.255: Landlord must install working smoke alarms; one in each bedroom area, outside sleeping area, on each level. Battery-operated permitted unless local code requires hardwired.
other_provisions.security_devices: §92.153: Landlord must install keyed deadbolts on exterior doors and window latches at commencement of lease, at tenant's request, or upon tenant's written request with 7-day notice.

Cleaning costs
