jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Virginia Code Title 55.1, Chapter 12 – Virginia Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
jurisdictions.notes: Virginia enacted significant tenant protections in 2021, 2023, and 2025. 2025 updates (Ch. 567, 684, 688) added COVID-era rental history protection and extended certain notice periods. HB 1419 (2021) added domestic violence lease termination rights. Just Cause eviction not required statewide but Fairfax County and Alexandria have local just cause requirements.
jurisdictions.full_text_summary: Virginia's VRLTA (Va. Code §§ 55.1-1200 to 55.1-1259) covers all residential tenancy aspects across 7 articles. Key sections include: 55.1-1200 (definitions), 55.1-1203 (application deposits), 55.1-1204.1 (fee disclosure), 55.1-1220 (habitability), 55.1-1226 (security deposit), 55.1-1245 (eviction), 55.1-1210 (landlord access), 55.1-1245.01 (retaliation).

security_deposit_section.full_text:
§ 55.1-1226. Security deposits.
A. Maximum deposit. A landlord shall not require a security deposit in an amount or value in excess of two months' periodic rent. Uses upon termination. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord may apply the security deposit to: (i) Any rent or late fees owed; (ii) Damage caused by the tenant under § 55.1-1227, excluding ordinary wear and tear; (iii) Other damage or charges as provided in the rental agreement; (iv) Actual damages caused by the tenant's breach of the rental agreement (§ 55.1-1251). Return deadline. The landlord shall return the security deposit, less lawful deductions, together with an itemized accounting of any deductions, within 45 days after the termination date or the date the tenant vacates, whichever is later.
B. Multiple tenants. Unless all tenants agree otherwise in writing, the refund shall be payable to all tenants as a single payment.
C. Utilities. A landlord may withhold a portion of the security deposit for third-party utility charges if written notice is provided at least 15 days before deposit processing.
D. Expedited processing. A landlord may offer expedited deposit processing for a fee if the tenant requests it in writing.
E. Deductions during tenancy. If a landlord makes deductions from the security deposit during the tenancy, the landlord must provide a written accounting within 30 days of the deduction. Failure to comply results in court ordering return of the deposit plus actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.
F. Records. A landlord shall maintain records of all security deposit deductions for two years and make them available for tenant inspection.
G. Move-out inspection. When a landlord requires a tenant to vacate, or within 5 days after receiving the tenant's notice to vacate, the landlord must notify the tenant of the right to be present at the move-out inspection. The inspection must occur within 72 hours of vacating. The landlord must then provide a written itemized list of damages.
H. Damage insurance alternative. A landlord may permit a tenant to provide damage insurance instead of a security deposit, provided the insurer is licensed by the Virginia State Corporation Commission and the policy meets specified requirements.
I. Tenant may convert. A tenant who initially chose damage insurance may convert to a security deposit at any time without the landlord changing the lease terms.

eviction_section.statutes: Va. Code §§ 55.1-1245, 55.1-1245 (two versions: current and effective July 1, 2028)

notice_requirements.nonpayment_of_rent: 5-day written notice to pay or vacate (current version); same with extended notice periods (2028 version)
notice_requirements.holdover_tenant: 30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy
notice_requirements.lease_violation: 21-day notice to cure for material breach; 30-day notice before termination
notice_requirements.criminal_activity: Immediate termination allowed for drug crimes or criminal acts threatening health/safety

eviction_section.full_text:
§ 55.1-1245. Noncompliance with rental agreement; monetary penalty.
A. Material breach. If the tenant commits a material breach of the rental agreement or a violation of § 55.1-1227 that materially affects health and safety, the landlord may give written notice specifying the tenant's acts or omissions and stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied within 21 days.
B. If the breach is remediable by repair or the payment of damages and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within 21 days, the rental agreement does not terminate.
C. Nonremediable breach. If the breach is nonremediable by repair or the payment of damages, the landlord may give written notice stating that the rental agreement will terminate no less than 30 days after receipt.
D. Immediate termination. If the tenant's breach consists of or constitutes a criminal or intentional act that is nonremediable and threatens health or safety, including drug-related criminal activity (as defined under § 54.1-3400), the landlord may terminate immediately and bring an action for possession. Court must hold first hearing within 15 calendar days of service.
E. Repeated breach. If a tenant who has previously received a written notice to remedy a breach and has remedied it commits the same type of breach again, the landlord may give 30 days' written notice to terminate.
F. Nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate within 5 days. For NSF checks, electronic payment failures, or bad faith stop payments, the tenant must pay within 5 days by cash, certified check, cashier's check, or completed electronic transfer.
J. COVID-19 Rental History Protection (2025). Landlords with more than 4 units may not take adverse action based solely on payment history or eviction records from March 12, 2020 through 30 days after the state of emergency ends. Applicants may dispute within 7 days. Violation carries $1,000 statutory damages plus attorney fees.

landlord_entry_section.full_text:
§ 55.1-1210. Landlord and tenant remedies for abuse of access.
If the tenant refuses to allow lawful access, the landlord may obtain injunctive relief to enforce access, or terminate the rental agreement. In either case, the landlord may recover actual damages and reasonable attorney fees.
If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or makes a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, or repeatedly demands entry even if lawful, the tenant may obtain injunctive relief, terminate the rental agreement, and recover actual damages and reasonable attorney fees.
§ 55.1-1229. Access; consent; correction of emergency conditions; tenant relocation; security systems.
(Governs tenant's obligation to allow landlord access and landlord's duties when entering; requires tenant not to unreasonably refuse access.)

habitability_section.full_text:
§ 55.1-1220. Landlord to maintain fit premises.
A. The landlord shall: (1) Comply with applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety; (2) Make all repairs necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (3) Keep common areas of multi-family premises clean and safe; (4) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air conditioning and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, provided by the landlord; (5) Maintain the premises to prevent accumulation of moisture and mold and remediate mold in accordance with § 8.01-226.12(E); (6) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal; (7) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times, and reasonable heating and air conditioning as appropriate for the season; (8) Provide certification that all smoke alarms are present, inspected, and in good working order, no more than once every 12 months.
B. Limitation of liability. The landlord is liable only for actual damages directly resulting from failure to exercise ordinary care.
C. Tenant-assigned duties. Landlord and tenant may agree in writing that the tenant performs certain obligations, provided the agreement is made in good faith and does not diminish the landlord's obligations to other tenants.

jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Virginia has no statewide rent control. Local governments are generally prohibited from imposing rent control. However, some localities (Fairfax County, Alexandria) have just cause eviction requirements for certain units.

late_fees_section.full_text:
§ 55.1-1204.1. Fee disclosure statement.
Every written rental agreement must include on the first page an itemized list of all fees charged to the tenant, including: (1) The security deposit; (2) The amount of rent due for each payment period; and (3) Any additional one-time fees due before the lease start date or included in the first rent payment. The following statement must appear prominently above the list: 'No additional security deposits or rent shall be charged unless they are listed below or incorporated into this agreement by way of a separate addendum after execution of this rental agreement.' Late fees must be reasonable and agreed upon in the lease. Virginia courts have held that excessive late fees may be deemed a penalty and unenforceable.

key_disclosures: § 55.1-1215: Mold disclosure – landlord must disclose known mold conditions.
§ 55.1-1216: Property sale disclosure – landlord must disclose when premises are listed for sale.
§ 55.1-1217: Military aviation facility disclosure – must disclose if near military aviation facility.
§ 55.1-1218: Defective drywall disclosure – must disclose known defective drywall.
§ 55.1-1219: Methamphetamine manufacturing disclosure – must disclose if premises were used to manufacture methamphetamine.
§ 55.1-1213: Security deposit transfer – must notify tenant when security deposit is transferred to new owner.

other_provisions.domestic_violence: § 55.1-1236: Victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, or other crimes may terminate the lease with 60 days notice if they provide a protective order or police report. The landlord may not terminate the lease solely based on domestic violence against the tenant.
other_provisions.military_termination: § 55.1-1235: Active duty military may terminate the rental agreement with 30 days written notice and a copy of military orders.
other_provisions.retaliation: § 55.1-1258: Retaliatory conduct by landlord is prohibited. If landlord takes adverse action within 6 months of tenant exercising rights, there is a presumption of retaliation.
other_provisions.abandonment: § 55.1-1249: Abandonment is presumed after 7 days if tenant fails to respond to landlord's written inquiry.

security_deposit.notes: Landlord must return deposit within 45 days with itemized list. No separate pet deposit cap.

rent_control.notes: Virginia prohibits local rent control. No statewide rent control.

landlord_entry.notes: No specific statutory advance notice requirement

habitability.notes: VA does not recognize implied warranty of habitability. Landlord must comply with building codes. No repair-and-deduct or rent withholding.

security_deposit.max_amount: No statutory limit (landlord may charge any amount)
allowable_deductions: Unpaid rent
Damage beyond normal wear and tear
Other lawful charges

eviction.estimated_timeline: 4-8 weeks uncontested (some courts have mandatory mediation)

lease_termination.month_to_month_notice_days: Landlord: 60 days; Tenant: 1 full rental period
lease_termination.penalty_for_early_termination: May owe remaining rent; landlord must mitigate

jurisdictions.primary_statutes: Revised Code of Washington (RCW) Chapter 59.18 – Residential Landlord-Tenant Act (RLTA); Chapter 59.12 – Unlawful Detainer; Chapter 59.20 – Mobile Home Landlord-Tenant Act
jurisdictions.last_updated: 2025 (2025 Legislative Session amendments; 2024 amendments including SB 6251 rent reporting; major 2021 reforms fully in effect)
jurisdictions.notes: Washington is one of the most tenant-protective states. RCW 59.18.650 requires Just Cause for all evictions statewide (enacted 2021). Security deposit return within 30 days with detailed documentation requirement. Landlord must provide 14-day rent arrears notice before filing for eviction. AG office offers statewide tenant mediation services. 2025 session added additional rent reporting protections.
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
RCW 59.18.260. SECURITY DEPOSIT – WRITTEN AGREEMENT REQUIRED.
(1) If a landlord requires a security deposit or damage deposit, the rental agreement MUST be in writing and MUST be specific as to which conditions allow the landlord to retain the deposit.
(2) The landlord shall provide a written inventory or checklist at the commencement of the rental agreement describing the condition and cleanliness of or existing defects in the unit, including but not limited to walls, floors, countertops, carpets, drapes, furniture, and appliances. The inventory must be signed and dated by the landlord and tenant. The tenant is entitled to a free copy.
(3) Each party must sign and date the inventory. Tenant may request one free replacement copy.
(4) The landlord shall not withhold a security deposit for normal wear and tear.
(5) If the landlord fails to provide the written checklist at move-in, the landlord shall not be entitled to withhold any portion of the security deposit for damages.

RCW 59.18.280. RETURN OF SECURITY DEPOSIT.
(1)(a) Within 30 days after the termination of the rental agreement and vacation of the premises, or within 30 days after abandonment of the premises under RCW 59.18.310, the landlord shall:
(i) Return the full deposit; OR
(ii) Provide a written statement of itemized deductions PLUS the balance of the deposit.
(b) If deductions are claimed, the landlord must provide documentation: for third-party repairs: estimates or paid invoices; for landlord/employee repairs: billing statements or invoices for materials, plus a statement of time spent and reasonable hourly rate.
(c) The landlord may NOT withhold for: (i) normal wear and tear; (ii) carpet cleaning unless tenant caused damage beyond normal use; (iii) repair/replacement of fixtures not documented in move-in checklist; (iv) costs exceeding actual damage.
(2) If landlord fails to provide itemized statement and return deposit within 30 days:
(a) Landlord forfeits the right to retain any portion;
(b) Court may award up to 2x the deposit as damages for intentional failure;
(c) Prevailing party entitled to attorney's fees and costs.
jurisdictions.rent_control_section: Washington has no statewide rent control. However, several cities have passed rent stabilization measures. Seattle adopted rent stabilization in November 2023 (King County Charter Amendment 6), effective 2025: Rent increases capped at CPI or 10% annually, whichever is lower, for most rentals in Seattle. Tacoma, Bellevue, and other cities do not have rent control. State preemption: Washington does not have a statewide preemption of local rent control laws, thus cities may enact rent stabilization.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section: Washington has no specific statewide cap on late fees for residential tenancies. RCW 59.18.283 requires any rent payment received to be applied first to rent. Common practice: late fees disclosed in lease, typically 5% of monthly rent after 5-day grace period. Note: Seattle Municipal Code §22.210.125 provides additional protections for Seattle tenants including late fee limits.
jurisdictions.disclosures_section:
RCW 59.18.060(12): Written fire safety disclosure required at lease signing.
RCW 59.18.060(13): Mold health hazards information required.
RCW 59.18.065: Landlord must disclose name and address of owner and property manager in writing.
RCW 59.18.570: Foreclosure notice required.
Federal lead paint disclosure required for pre-1978 housing.
RCW 59.18.285: If non-refundable fee charged, must be stated as non-refundable in writing.

other_provisions.just_cause_2021: RCW 59.18.650 enacted 2021, makes Washington first state to require just cause for ALL evictions (not just rent-controlled units). Major tenants rights advance.
other_provisions.14_day_pay_notice: RCW 59.18.057: Before filing eviction for nonpayment, landlord must serve 14-day Pay or Vacate notice with specific content including tenant resources.
other_provisions.domestic_violence: RCW 59.18.575: Victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, unlawful harassment, or stalking may terminate lease with 20 days written notice and documentation.
other_provisions.retaliation: RCW 59.18.240: Landlord may not retaliate against tenant who asserts rights, complains to government, or organizes. Retaliation presumed if adverse action within 90 days of protected activity.
other_provisions.security_deposit_return_documentation: RCW 59.18.280: Detailed documentation requirements for withheld deposits (invoices, photos since AB 2801-equivalent). Courts strictly enforce.
other_provisions.seattle_local_rules: Seattle has additional protections: relocation assistance for certain no-fault evictions; rental inspection program; source of income discrimination ban; 12-month eviction notice for certain demolition/renovation.
other_provisions.application_fees: RCW 59.18.257: Landlord must provide written notice of screening criteria before charging application fee. Landlord must provide written denial notice. Application fee must be refunded if unit rented to other applicant before screening completed.
other_provisions.early_termination_military: RCW 59.18.220: Active duty military may terminate lease with 30 days notice.

security_deposit.notes: Landlord must provide written checklist at move-in. Written rental agreement required if deposit taken. Pet deposit separate from security deposit.

rent_control.notes: Washington prohibits statewide rent control but allows local rent control. Seattle, Tacoma, and other cities have local ordinances.

landlord_entry.notes: Landlord must give at least 2 days written notice before entering except in emergencies

habitability.notes: Strong tenant protections; landlord must keep premises fit for habitation.

jurisdictions.title: Wisconsin Residential Landlord-Tenant Law
jurisdictions.governing_statutes: Wis. Stat. §§ 704.01–704.95; Wis. Admin. Code ATCP 134
jurisdictions.security_deposit_section:
Wis. Stat. § 704.05; ATCP 134.01–134.09

Key provisions:
- Maximum amount: NO STATUTORY LIMIT. Landlords may charge any amount.
- Return deadline: 21 days after tenancy ends.
- Itemization required: Landlord must provide an itemized list of deductions.
- Permissible deductions: Unpaid rent, damage beyond normal wear and tear, amounts owed under the lease.
- Separate account: Not required by state law (but see local ordinances).
- Record keeping: Landlord must keep records of deposit and deductions.
- Tenant's forwarding address: Tenant must provide forwarding address to receive refund.
- Penalty for non-compliance: Tenant may recover up to twice the amount of the deposit if landlord fails to comply.
- Security deposit defined: Any payment to a landlord to secure performance of a rental agreement.
- Pre-rental payments: Any payment used as a security deposit is subject to these rules.

Wis. Stat. § 704.05(1): 'Any payment to a landlord to secure performance of a rental agreement shall be called a security deposit regardless of how it is described.'
Wis. Stat. § 704.05(2): 'A landlord shall return a tenant's security deposit within 21 days after the tenancy ends.'
Wis. Stat. § 704.05(3): 'If a landlord retains any portion of the security deposit, the landlord shall provide the tenant with a written statement itemizing the deductions within the time period provided.'
ATCP 134.02: Requires landlords to disclose the location of the tenant's security deposit and provide receipts.
jurisdictions.eviction_section:
Wis. Stat. §§ 799.40–799.45; Ch. 704

Key provisions:
- Judicial eviction required: Yes. All evictions must go through court.
- Nonpayment of rent notice: 5 days written notice (with opportunity to cure for first violation in a rental period).
- Lease violation notice: 14 days written notice with 14-day right to cure.
- Month-to-month termination: 28 days written notice required.
- Holdover (overstaying): 5 days notice before filing if tenant remains after lease expires.
- Self-help eviction prohibited: Landlords may NOT lock out tenants, shut off utilities, or remove tenant's belongings without a court order.
- Timeline: Uncontested eviction takes approximately 3-6 weeks from filing to judgment.
- Court process: Landlord files in circuit court; tenant is served with summons and complaint; tenant has right to appear and defend.
- Eviction types: Nonpayment, lease violation, holdover, nuisance/illegal activity.
- Failure to pay: Court may enter judgment for possession and unpaid rent.
Wis. Stat. § 704.27: Governs termination of tenancy for nonpayment of rent. Landlord must provide at least 5 days notice before commencing action.
Wis. Stat. § 704.44: No self-help evictions permitted.
jurisdictions.habitability_section:
Wis. Stat. § 704.07; Wis. Stat. § 66.1015

Key provisions:
- Implied warranty of habitability: Wisconsin recognizes a duty of landlords to maintain fit premises.
- Landlord's duty: To keep premises in a fit and habitable condition, comply with applicable building and housing codes, make repairs, keep common areas clean and safe, maintain essential services.
- Tenant's remedies for breach: (1) Repair and deduct (see below); (2) Terminate the rental agreement if conditions substantially impair the health or safety of the tenant; (3) Bring an action to enforce the landlord's duty.
- Repair and deduct: ALLOWED. Wis. Stat. § 704.07(2): If landlord fails to repair conditions that substantially affect health or safety after notice, tenant may have repairs made and deduct cost from rent (reasonable amount, not exceeding $300 or one month's rent, whichever is greater).
- Rent withholding: NOT ALLOWED. Tenant may not unilaterally withhold rent for habitability issues.
- Retaliatory eviction: PROHIBITED. Wis. Stat. § 704.45. A landlord may not evict a tenant because the tenant has complained to a government agency or exercised rights under the rental agreement.
- Local codes: Cities like Milwaukee have additional habitability requirements under local ordinances.
- Health/safety issues: Examples include no heat, no running water, broken locks, severe pest infestation, exposed wiring, major water leaks.
jurisdictions.late_fees_section:
Wis. Stat. § 704.05; ATCP 134.03

Key provisions:
- Late fees: Allowed if specified in the written rental agreement.
- Amount: No statutory maximum, but must be reasonable and not excessive.
- Wisconsin law allows landlords to charge reasonable late fees.
- Grace period: No state-mandated grace period, but landlords typically allow 3-5 days.
- Written agreement required: Late fees must be specified in the lease/rental agreement.
- ATCP 134.03(2): A landlord may charge a late fee only if the amount is specified in the rental agreement and the fee reflects the landlord's actual costs of processing late payments.
jurisdictions.landlord_entry_section:
Wis. Stat. § 704.14

Key provisions:
- Reasonable notice required: Generally 12 hours to 24 hours advance notice is considered reasonable.
- No statutory minimum notice: Wisconsin does not specify a minimum number of hours in statute, but courts have found 24 hours to be reasonable.
- Entry for repairs: Landlord may enter to make repairs, inspections, or to show the premises.
- Entry to exhibit: Landlord may enter to exhibit the premises to prospective tenants, purchasers, or lenders during the last 30 days of tenancy.
- Emergency entry: Landlord may enter without notice in case of emergency.
- Tenant's rights: Tenant may restrict entry by locking certain areas only if specified in the rental agreement.
- Reasonable times: Entry should be during normal business hours or reasonable hours.
Wis. Stat. § 704.14: Governs landlord's right of entry. Landlord may enter the dwelling unit to inspect, repair, show, or in case of emergency.
jurisdictions.rent_increase_section:
Wis. Stat. § 66.1015

Key provisions:
- Rent control: PROHIBITED statewide. Wis. Stat. § 66.1015 prohibits any municipality from enacting rent control.
- Rent increases: No statutory limit on amount or frequency for month-to-month tenancies.
- Notice required: For month-to-month tenancies, at least 28 days advance written notice of a rent increase.
- Fixed-term leases: Rent cannot be increased during the lease term unless the lease specifically allows it.
- Just cause: Not required for rent increases in Wisconsin.
- Early termination penalties: A landlord may not charge a penalty for early termination that exceeds the landlord's actual damages.
